Whether or not IC 5273 is losing some of its gas to the intra-group medium, we note that some ram-pressure stripping is definitely happening in this group, as demonstrated by for NGC 7421. Their ATCA data show that \ at a column density below 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 is clearly being pushed off the galaxy in a eastward direction, consistent with the compressed, star-forming west side of the optical disc (Fig. fig:cutouts0 ). The column density sensitivity of our data is not sufficient to study this object in more detail, but the slight offset between \ and optical emission visible in Fig. fig:cutouts0 is in agreement with the deeper ATCA image.

gmw_00004276.name: IC 5273
gmw_00004276.overview: IC 5273 is a late-type barred spiral galaxy that represents one of the brightest neutral hydrogen sources in its group [arXiv:1912.07452, arXiv:1506.04399]. The galaxy exhibits an off-center gas distribution relative to its stellar disk, which may indicate environmental interactions or ram pressure stripping [arXiv:1912.07452, arXiv:1506.04399].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5273 as a distant barred spiral with blue outer colors, strong HI emission, and off-center gas suggesting environmental interaction or stripping.

quotes.quote: IC 5273 This late-type barred spiral is the third brightest galaxy of the group ( M_g=-21.59 mag). It is the most distant galaxy in projection from IC 1459 ( 79 2 ), and it has the larger relative velocity to IC 1459 ( v_ rel =-509 17 km s ^ 1 ). The bar is clearly visible (Figure fig:ic5273sp ) both in the colour composite image and in the surface-brightness radial profile ( 6 R 40 ). The average colours are g-=0.54 mag and g-i=1.05 mag, and the colour profiles have a bluer decline for R>3 . It is also one of the brightest sources in ASKAP HI, M _ HI =(5.4 1.1) 10^ 9 M _ Serra2015 .

There is an off-centre between the HI gas and stellar disk, and the HI distribution has a south-east asymmetry opposite to the direction of IC 1459. The galaxy could have suffered some interactions with the environment or from ram pressure stripping, as for NGC 7421 Serra2015 .

sources.summary: The paper presents IC 5273 as a bright H I detection with possible interaction signatures, where outer gas may be displaced northwest relative to the stellar body.

quotes.quote: Excluding IC 5270 and NGC 7418 -- which we discuss in Sec. sec:ic5270 - the brightest sources in the ASKAP \ mosaic cube are ESO 406-G42, IC 5269B, IC 5273 and NGC 7418A. These objects have an \ mass of a few times 10^9 \ and a fairly typical \ spectrum. Among them, ESO 406-G42 stands out as one in relatively poor agreement with the Parkes spectra. Its \ velocity field appears noisy, too. This is explained by the position of this galaxy close to the edge of the observed field, in a region where the noise is 50 percent higher than in the inner region of the mosaic. Some discrepancy between ASKAP and Parkes spectra can also be seen at the high-velocity end of the \ spectra of IC 5269B and IC 5273.

Another galaxy that may be undergoing some interaction with the group environment is IC 5273. Its \ morphology is suggestive of gas near the disc edge being pushed north-west relative to the stellar body. However, the signal in the gas disc outer regions is very low, and deeper, higher-resolution data would be needed to clarify the situation.

gmw_00004277.name: IC 5269
gmw_00004277.overview: IC 5269 is a barred lenticular galaxy that is a member of a galaxy group [arXiv:1912.07452]. It features distinct bulge and stellar disk components, but it lacks any associated neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:1912.07452].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5269 as a barred lenticular, small massive early-type group member with clear bulge and disk components and no associated HI.

quotes.quote: IC 5269 This is a barred lenticular galaxy with an average colour ( g-=0.77 0.25 mag) consistent with the predicted colour of ETGs LaBarbera2012 . It is the forth most massive ( 3.32 10^ 10 M _ ) and smallest bright galaxy ( R_ e ,g =2.70 kpc) galaxy of the group. From the surface brightness profiles there is a clear evidence for the bulge and stellar disk components. Its colour profile is almost flat outside of the seeing-dominated region. It does not have any associated HI (Figure fig:ic5269sp ).

gmw_00004278.name: IC 5269B
gmw_00004278.overview: IC 5269B is a low-mass, highly inclined disk galaxy located near IC 1459 [arXiv:1912.07452]. It features blue outer regions and an HI gas component that is off-center relative to its stellar disk, which may indicate a past galaxy-galaxy or galaxy-environment interaction [arXiv:1912.07452].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5269B as a low-mass, inclined galaxy near IC 1459 with blue outer regions and off-center HI possibly produced by interaction.

quotes.quote: IC 5269B This is the second closest galaxy in projection to IC 1459 ( 14 5 ). It is the second less bright, M_g=-19.60 mag, and less massive, 5.0 10^ 9 M _ , bright galaxy of the group. This galaxy is very inclined with a bluer outer regions (Figure fig:ic5269Bsp ). There is an off-centre of the HI component respect to the stellar disk, that seems to be more elongated in the opposite direction. This could be due to some galaxy-galaxy or galaxy-environment interaction, providing a tidal stripping component and this gas might build-up the debris structure around IC 1459.

gmw_00004279.name: 4C + 29.48
gmw_00004279.overview: The radio source 4C + 29.48 is associated with a gamma-ray source [arXiv:1801.03124] and contains a blazar at a redshift of 1.142 [arXiv:1801.03124]. This blazar component exhibits a core-jet structure typical of flat-spectrum radio quasars [arXiv:1302.6040], while the other radio complexes in the field resemble active galactic nuclei lobes [arXiv:1801.03124].

sources.summary: The paper resolves 4C + 29.48 into three radio complexes, identifying the northern complex as a redshift 1.142 blazar and arguing the other steep-spectrum complexes are unrelated lobes or lensed images.

quotes.quote: The ray source 3FGL J1323.0+2942 is associated with the radio source 4C + 29.48 and classified as a blazar of unknown type, lacking optical spectrum and redshift. The higher-resolution radio data showed that 4C + 29.48 comprises three bright radio-emitting features located within a 1' diameter area. We aim to reveal their nature and pinpoint the origin of the ray emission.

According to the VLBI data, the northernmost complex of 4C + 29.48 contains a blazar with a high brightness temperature compact core and a steep-spectrum jet feature. The blazar is positionally coincident with an optical source at a redshift of 1.142 . Its mid-infrared colors also support its association with a ray emitting blazar. The two other radio complexes have steep radio spectra similar to AGN-related lobes and do not have optical or infrared counterparts in currently available surveys.

sources.summary: The paper uses quasar 1320+299 (4C 29.48) as a VLBI test target; only component A is detected, showing a core-jet structure typical of flat-spectrum radio quasars.

quotes.quote: The target field selected was that of the quasar 1320+299 ( 4C 29.48 ).

Component A was the only one detected by the present observations. The image we obtained of component A ( fig:aselfcal ) shows the core-jet structure usually found in flat spectrum radio quasars. This finding confirms the interpretation suggested by Cornwell \ considering their observations made at lower resolution, in which the A component also shows a core-jet structure with the core presenting a flat spectral index.

gmw_00004280.name: Sunrise Arc
gmw_00004280.overview: The Sunrise Arc is a highly magnified galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z = 6.2 [arXiv:2208.09007]. It is gravitationally lensed by the massive galaxy cluster WHL J013719.8-082841 and hosts the highly magnified star Earendel [arXiv:2208.09007].

sources.summary: The paper describes the Sunrise Arc as Earendel's z~6.2, highly magnified host galaxy, deriving component photometric redshifts and adopting z=6.2 for further lensing analysis.

quotes.quote: Earendel is in a = 6.2 0.1 galaxy dubbed the Sunrise Arc, the most highly magnified galaxy at z 6 Salmon2020 . It is lensed by a massive z = 0.566 galaxy cluster WHL J013719.8--082841 (henceforth ; RA = 01:37:25.0, Dec = 08:27:23, J2000; WHL12,WenHan15 ).

This splits the Sunrise Arc up into a total of 4 components, and gives photometric redshifts of = 6.00^ +0.09 _ 0.11 , = 6.31^ +0.08 _ 0.15 , = 6.04^ +0.20 _ 0.23 , and = 6.25^ +0.12 _ 0.16 for each component. Combining these redshifts yields a total redshift estimate for the full arc of = 6.15^ +0.27 _ 0.34 . Previous works Salmon2020,Welch2022_earendel have used other SED fitting methods to estimate the photometric redshift of the Sunrise Arc using only the \ imaging. These results consistently found redshifts = 6.2 0.1 , consistent with the present fit. We thus adopt a fiducial redshift of z = 6.2 for the Sunrise Arc, and by extension Earendel, which we use for all further calculations in this paper.

gmw_00004281.name: IGR J2018+4043
aliases_external: Swift XRT counterpart J2018 / IGR J2018+4043
gmw_00004281.overview: IGR J2018+4043 is a strongly obscured accreting source that is likely an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0609676]. Multiwavelength observations of the source reveal counterparts in the radio, infrared, optical, and X-ray bands [arXiv:astro-ph/0609676].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J2018+4043 with a hard Swift source and multiwavelength counterparts, arguing that its absorbed spectrum and extended optical/IR appearance make it likely an obscured AGN.

quotes.quote: We report here the results of recent 1.5 Ms FCFOV observations of the source with \ ( 2.1) and the detection of a counterpart of IGR J2018+4043 in the 0.5--10 keV band with Swift XRT ( 2.2). The accurate positioning of the Swift source allowed us to find its possible radio/IR/optical counterparts in archival data ( 2.3 and 2.4). Based on these multiwavelength observations, we suggest that the source is a strongly obscured accreting source, likely an AGN, and discuss the possibility that the EGRET source is indeed its ray counterpart ( 3).

Based on the positional coincidence and spectral compatibility, we conclude that we detected a Swift counterpart of IGR J2018+4043; we will call this source J2018 hereafter. Having the position of J2018 well constrained by the \ observations, we looked for counterparts of the X-ray source in archival IR/optical data.

gmw_00004282.name: SBS 1419+538
gmw_00004282.overview: SBS 1419+538 is a bright quasar located at a redshift of z = 1.862 [arXiv:2109.12154]. Spectropolarimetric observations of its Mg II spectral band have been used to estimate the mass of its central supermassive black hole [arXiv:2109.12154].

sources.summary: The paper presents Mg II spectropolarimetry of the quasar SBS 1419+538, deriving a supermassive black hole mass and comparing it with virial and literature estimates.

quotes.quote: Here we present black hole measurements of SBS 1419+538 using spectropolarimetric observations in the Mg II spectral band. The observations are performed by 6m telescope of SAO RAS using SCORPIO-2. We found a good agreement for the estimated supermassive black hole (SMBH) mass for this object using spectropolarimetry when compared with the mass obtained using other methods.

In this work, we report the first spectropolarimetric observations of the Mg II line for a distant AGN SBS 1419+538 and we compare the SMBH mass estimated using the 2015ApJ...800L..35A method with the estimates provided by different authors using other methods (most notably reverberation mapping). In order to test the model of polarization changes for the Mg II line, in February 2019 we carried out the spetropolarimetric observations of the quasar SBS 1419+538. SBS 1419+538 (RA 14 21 06.9 Dec +53 37 45.2, J2000) is a bright quasar (16.8 mag in the g-sdss band) at the redshift z = 1.862 determined for the first time via the Second Byurakan Survey sbs2 .

gmw_00004283.name: PKS0227-369
gmw_00004283.overview: PKS0227-369 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1212.2632] and an extremely Compton-dominant Fermi blazar [arXiv:1303.1182]. Its multi-wavelength spectrum is fitted by a high-power, high-bulk-Lorentz-factor jet observed close to the line of sight [arXiv:1212.2632, arXiv:1303.1182].

sources.summary: The paper fits a M87-scaled accelerating jet model to PKS0227-369, finding good radio-to-high-energy fits when equipartition begins outside the BLR or in the dusty torus and requiring a powerful, aligned jet.

quotes.quote: We fit our model to simultaneous multi-wavelength observations of the Compton-dominant FSRQ type blazar PKS0227-369, with a jet geometry set by M87 and an accelerating bulk Lorentz factor consistent with simulations and theory. We investigate models in which the jet comes into equipartition at different distances along the jet and equipartition is maintained via the conversion of jet bulk kinetic energy into particle acceleration.

The model fits the observations, including radio data, very well if the jet comes into equipartition outside the BLR within the dusty torus (1.5pc) or at further distances (34pc). The fits require a high power jet with a large bulk Lorentz factor observed close to the line of sight, consistent with our expectations for a Compton-dominant blazar. We find that our fit in which the jet comes into equipartition furthest along the jet, which has a jet with the geometry of M87 scaled linearly with black hole mass, has an inferred black hole mass close to previous estimates. This implies that the jet of PKS0227 might be well described by the same jet geometry as M87.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS0227-369 as an extremely Compton-dominant Fermi blazar whose spectrum is fitted by a powerful, high-Lorentz-factor jet near the line of sight.

quotes.quote: We used this model to successfully fit to the quiescent spectrum of the most Compton-dominant object in the FERMI sample PKS0227-369 ( ), using a jet geometry fixed by the radio observations of M87 ( ) scaled linearly with black hole mass. We found that the fit to PKS0227 required a high power, high bulk Lorentz factor jet observed close to the line of sight, consistent with our expectations from the blazar sequence ( ) and unification ( ). We found that the spectrum of PKS0227 was well fitted by a jet which comes into equipartition outside of the BLR; within the dusty torus or at a larger distance along the jet.

gmw_00004284.name: NGC 4173
gmw_00004284.overview: NGC 4173 is a small, edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9811096, arXiv:1401.4170] located in the foreground of the Hickson Compact Group 61 [arXiv:1401.4170]. It features a relatively large ultraviolet halo [arXiv:1401.4170] that can be modeled using only a typical thin dust disk [arXiv:1511.03789].

sources.summary: The survey treats NGC 4173 as a 5 arcmin edge-on system whose published H I line parameters disagree, rejecting one Arecibo velocity and noting width discrepancies.

quotes.quote: Overall, the agreement between the measurements obtained from the driftscan survey and independent pointed observations are very satisfactory with a few discrepancies discussed below: No. 12 (NGC 4173): The \ line parameters of this 5 ' diameter edge-on system measured at Arecibo by Williams Rood (1986) and independently by Bothun (1985) are quite different from the other available values.

A comparison of the difference between systemic velocities (actually, the centre velocities of the line profiles) measured at \ and elsewhere shows no significant dependence on radial velocity, with the exception of one data point for NGC 4173 (No. 12). Here we consider the radial velocity of 1020  \ measured at Arecibo by Williams Rood (1986) as spurious, seen the agreement between the 3 other measured values.

A comparison of the difference between the W_ 50 and W_ 20 \ line widths measured at \ and elsewhere shows a good correlation as well; the largest discrepancy is found between the survey values for the 5 ' diameter edge-on system NGC 4173 (No. 12) and the Arecibo W_ 50 and W_ 20 measurements by Williams Rood (1986) as well as by Bothun (1985), while the other pointed observations of this object are consistent with ours.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4173 as a small foreground spiral with a relatively large UV halo, measurable only on one side because of nearby HCG 061 members.

quotes.quote: IC 5249, NGC 24, NGC 6503, and NGC 4173 These galaxies are significantly smaller than the other spiral galaxies in our sample, and were included because they met our cutoff criteria and had good data. NGC 4173 also has a relatively large halo, assuming it is indeed at 8 Mpc, where its M_K = -18 mag indicates a stellar mass of M_ star 3 10^8 M_ . We are only able to measure the halo emission to one side of NGC 4173 because of the members of HCG 061 to the south; NGC 4173 was originally classified as a member of HCG 061 (HCG 061b), but it is known to be a foreground object.

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 4173 among targets where a thick dust disk is questionable; it has low thick-dust scale height and its UV halo can be modeled with a thin disk alone.

quotes.quote: However, it is found that the rest three targets (IC 5249, NGC 24, and NGC 4173) do not necessarily need a thick dust disk to model the ultraviolet (UV) halo, because its contribution is too small and the UV halo may be caused by the wing part of the point spread function.
NGC 4173 has the lowest z_d^ thick as does IC 5249, and has the lowest .

However, we found that the UV halo of three targets (IC 5249, NGC 24, and NGC 4173) can be modeled with the typical thin dust disk solely, since the contribution from the thick dust disk is too small.

gmw_00004285.name: FRII radio galaxy
aliases_external: a0, the FRII core / LUCI source l1
FRII galaxy near SDSS J1030+0524 field
gmw_00004285.overview: The FRII radio galaxy J103025+052430 is a massive starburst galaxy located at the center of a galaxy protocluster at redshift z=1.7 [arXiv:2302.11977, arXiv:2112.03658]. It hosts a heavily obscured, Compton-thick active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2112.03658, arXiv:2008.13665] and features a large molecular gas reservoir rotating perpendicularly to its radio jets [arXiv:2112.03658].

sources.summary: The paper presents J103025+052430 as a powerful z=1.7 giant FRII radio galaxy in a protocluster, using LOFAR and Chandra data to study large-scale AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: J103025+052430 is a powerful ( P_ 1400 MHz =4 10^ 26 \ W/Hz ) Fanaroff–Riley II (FRII, fanaroff1974 ) radio galaxy, associated with a massive galaxy ( M_* 3 10^ 11 ) located at the center of a galaxy protocluster at redshift z=1.6987 ( petric2003, nanni2018, gilli2019, damato2020, damato2021, damato2022 . It is powered by a supermassive black hole with mass <3.2 10^7 \ as estimated by based on the bolometric luminosity. With its total projected extension ( 700 kpc), it is one of the few giant radio sources at z>1.5 studied to date (see e.g. erlund2008, kuzmicz2021 for other examples), providing a unique opportunity to study feedback from AGN jets at high redshift, on large (hundreds of kpc) scales.

The total flux density of J103025+052430 at 144 MHz and 1400 MHz, as well as the flux densities of the individual morphological components, are listed in Table tab:flux , together with the respective luminosities. These were measured from the 6-arcsec resolution maps at both frequencies for consistency and using the black regions shown in Fig. fig:spec , right panel, which were drawn following the 3 contours at 144 MHz. The total uncertainty ( S ) on the flux densities ( S ) were computed by combining in quadrature the flux density scale calibration uncertainty ( S_c ) and the image rms noise ( ) multiplied by the flux density integration area in beam units ( A_ int ): S = (S S_c)^2+( A_ int )^2 htp htp 0.5 0.50 The integrated spectral indices and k-corrected radio powers are also presented in Table tab:flux . The spectral index uncertainties were estimated with the following formula based on the propagation of error: = 1 ln 144 1400 S_ 144MHz S_ 144MHz ^2+ S_ 1400MHz S_ 1400MHz ^2 where S_ 144MHz and S_ 1400MHz are the flux density values at the respective frequencies in MHz and S_ 144MHz and S_ 1400MHz their corresponding uncertainty. With a power of P_ 144 MHz 3 10^ 27 W/Hz, J103025+052430 appears to be among the 30 most powerful giant radio sources (with P_ 144 MHz >10^ 27 W/Hz) in the sample of 239 GRGs and GRQs (optically selected, see duncan2019 ) extracted by from LoTSS.

With its broad multi-band coverage, the giant radio galaxy J103025+052430 at z=1.7, represents an exceptional laboratory where to test how AGN jet feedback influences the growth of galaxies at high redshift and the evolution of the ICM before virialisation. In this paper we have reported new LOFAR observations at 144 MHz of the system, which we have used in combination with already published VLA observations at 1.4 GHz damato2021, damato2022 and 0.5-7 keV Chandra observations gilli2019 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the FRII high-redshift radio galaxy J103023+052426, finding a massive starburst proto-BCG host and possible positive AGN feedback.

quotes.quote: This structure comprises at least 10 star-forming galaxies (SFGs) distributed around a powerful Fanaroff--Riley type II (FRII; ) HzRG at RA= 10^h 30^m 25^s.2 , Dec= +5^ 24' 28''.7 , part of the NRAO VLA Sky Survey (NVSS, ID: J103023+052426; ) and hosting a heavily obscured AGN (X-ray derived column density N_ H,X 10^ 24   cm^ 2 ; ).

Based on Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA) observations of the CO(2--1) transition line, ref. reported the discovery of three new gas-rich members (magenta circles in Figure  fig:alma_cont ), in addition to a large molecular gas reservoir ( M_ H_2 2 10^ 11 M_ ) distributed in a 20--30 kpc structure around the FRII host galaxy and rotating perpendicularly to the radio jets (at least in projection). Furthermore, ref. demonstrated that the overdensity will collapse in a cluster of at least 10^ 14 M_ at z = 0, and that the FRII galaxy will likely evolve into the future brightest cluster galaxy (BCG).

We derive: L_ bol, AGN 1.1 10^ 46 erg/s , L_ 8-1000  m 3.7 10^ 12 L_ (24 of which is ascribed to the AGN), stellar mass M_ 3.7 10^ 11 M_ and SFR = 570 M_ /yr. The high sSFR = 1.5 0.5 Gyr^ 1 derived for the FRII host galaxy classifies this object as a starburst galaxy when compared with other SFGs at the same redshift schreiber_2015 , implying that the proto-BCG is currently being observed during the brief phase (few 10^8 yr; ref. ) of its major stellar mass building. Indeed, we note that the derived SFR implies that the molecular gas reservoir measured by damato_2020b will be depleted in 3.5 10^8 yr.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a foreground FRII radio galaxy by coordinates, with an obscured nucleus, asymmetric radio lobes, and uncertain links to the diffuse X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: In the radio observation at 1.4 GHz taken by the Very Large Array (down to a 3 limit of 60 Jy/beam; Pet03 ), we found a radio lobe of a FRII galaxy (RA = 10:30:25.19, Dec = +5:24:28.50; Pet03 ) inside our region (see Figure 8, top).

The nucleus of the FRII coincides with a Chandra source detected only above 2 keV (with 30 net counts), suggesting an extremely obscured nucleus (see Figure 8, top). The Eastern lobe of the FRII has a total radio flux of 1.7 mJy and falls within the region of diffuse emission, while the Western one is much brighter, with a total radio flux of 24 mJy. No X-ray emission is associated with the Western lobe. A radio jet is also seen running from the radio core to the Eastern lobe.

sources.summary: The paper describes the central FRII radio galaxy as Compton-thick, gas rich, massive, star forming, and likely the progenitor of the future brightest cluster galaxy.

quotes.quote: The overdensity includes a powerful Compton-thick Fanaroff-Riley type II (FRII) radio galaxy, around which we discovered a large molecular gas reservoir ( M_ H2 2 10^ 11 M_ ). We fit the FRII resolved CO emission with a 2D Gaussian model with a major (minor) axis of 27 ( 17) kpc, which is a factor of 3 larger than the optical rest-frame emission. The FRII is located at the center of the projected spatial distribution of the structure members, and its velocity offset from the peak of the redshift distribution is well within the velocity dispersion of the structure. All this, coupled with the large amount of gas around the FRII, its stellar mass of 3 10^ 11 M_ , star formation rate of 200-600 M_ /yr, and powerful radio-to-X-ray emission, suggests that this source is the likely progenitor of the future brightest cluster galaxy.

gmw_00004286.name: a1
gmw_00004286.overview: The galaxy A1 is a gas-rich member of a large-scale structure, situated westward of an FRII radio core [arXiv:2008.13665]. It is located within 80 kpc of two neighboring galaxies, a0 and a2, and has a derived star-formation rate based on its gas density [arXiv:2008.13665].

sources.summary: The paper identifies A1 as a lensed background galaxy forming a blue arc, measures its spectroscopic redshift and absorption/emission features, and reconstructs it as a caustic-distorted source.

quotes.quote: The cluster also lenses other background galaxies, the most prominent of which is seen as a blue arc south of the cluster core (labeled A1 in Figure fig.hst ). In addition, we measured the spectroscopic redshifts of several cluster member galaxies, and of the lensed galaxy A1 at z= . Stark et al. (2013) also measure the spectra of the quasar, z=2.807 and of galaxy A1.

Lensed galaxy A1 : From two slitlets in Mask 2, we confirm the known arc redshift of z= \ from ISM absorption lines Fe II 2344, 2382; Fe II 2586,2600; and MgII 2798, 2803. We identify weak nebular emission of Si III ] 1892, and C III ] 1909.
Galaxy A1 is highly distorted by the lensing potential due to its close proximity to the caustic. It is likely that a small region of this galaxy is multiply imaged within the giant arc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAE A1 as a faint z=2.7659 Ly-alpha emitter 87 kpc from the QSO sightline, near low-metallicity absorbing gas unlikely to be a local outflow.

quotes.quote: There are three foreground LAEs at d<300 kpc from the QSO sightline. Two of these objects, LAE A1 at peak redshift z_ peak = 2.7659 and LAE A2 at z_ peak = 2.7713 , are closely situated in redshift space with their Ly emission peaks separated by merely v 400 . In addition, both LAEs are situated on opposite sides of the QSO at a projected separation of 248 kpc, with the QSO sightline probing the LAE A1 at d=87 kpc and LAE A2 at d=161 kpc (top-left panel of Figure 3). A third emitter, LAE B1, occurs at z_ peak =2.7483 , and is probed by the background QSO at d=187 kpc (top-left panel of Figure 4).

The observed Ly line fluxes of the three LAEs are F_ Ly = (2.7 0.4) 10^ 18 , (3.3 0.6) 10^ 18 , and (3.5 0.5) 10^ 18 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 for LAEs A1, A2, and B1 (R08). These observed line fluxes correspond to Ly luminosities of L_ Ly =(1.7-2.2) 10^ 41 erg s^ 1 . The inferred Ly luminosities of the three LAEs are a factor of 20-30 lower than the characteristic luminosity of the LAE luminosity function at z 3 (e.g., Cassata \ 2011; Konno \ 2016; Drake \ 2017), which indicate that these LAEs are intrinsically faint compared to the general LAE population at the same epoch. This conclusion would stand even if the observations were subject to significant slit losses of 50 (e.g., R08; Cassata \ 2011).
Here we focus our discussion on Complex A, which is found closest to LAE A1 at d=87 kpc.

sources.summary: The Cross is a galaxy-scale strong lens at z=0.8106 with a z=3.4 source, large Einstein radius, external shear, and an overdense local environment tied to a z~0.8 sheet.

quotes.quote: In addition to a previously-known Einstein Cross also found by our search (the ``Cross,'' , with z_ lens =0.8106 and a published z_ source =3.40 ), we identify two new strong galaxy-galaxy lenses with multiple extended arcs. The first, (the ``Dewdrop''; z_ lens =0.5798 ), lenses two distinct extended sources into two pairs of arcs ( z_ source =0.9818 by nebular [ O II ] emission), while the second, (the ``Anchor''; z_ lens =0.4625 ), produces a single pair of arcs (source redshift not yet known). Four less convincing arc/counter-arc and two-image lens candidates are also found and presented for completeness. All three definite lenses are fit reasonably well by simple singular isothermal ellipsoid models including external shear, giving ^2_ values close to unity.

The lens redshift is z_ lens =0.8106 (Table  tab:lenses ), and the source is at z_ source =3.4 crampton:96 . The large Einstein radius _ E =1.447 arcsec and the four-image configuration require a large enclosed mass and a significant amount of external shear, _ mod =0.080 , a result consistent with treu:04 . The best-fit model shows very small residuals at the two outer images, a feature corrected for by treu:04 with a potential gradient that is presumably associated with a nearby structure.

The Cross is in a fairly overdense local environment, which is consistent with this lens being associated with the z 0.8 sheet described in koo:96 and im:02 . Given this, the shear of 10 required by the model (see also treu:04 ) seems plausible.

sources.summary: The paper describes G39.1105-0.0160 as a compact IR-Quiet radio galaxy candidate whose proper motion leaves either an extragalactic radio-source or slow Galactic-source interpretation possible.

quotes.quote: Inconclusive G39.1105-0.0160 (A 1) was detected in two epochs in our survey and the total proper motion is only measured at a < 2 significance level (See Figure motionfits1 and Table vlba_survey_pm ). B10 did not detect this target in the infrared, optical and X-ray catalogues available at the time, and a Vizier search in the current available catalogues also did not show any matches for the source at any other wavelength. The THOR survey estimates a preliminary spectral index of 0.15 0.1 for A 1, which is consistent with the source being a flat spectrum source.

The target is detected as a compact source and the lower limit of the brightness temperature is > 3 10 ^6 K. The CORNISH classification of this source is an IR-Quiet radio galaxy. Based on just the proper motion, A 1 could be an extragalactic radio source, or a Galactic radio source with a slow projected speed.

sources.summary: The paper describes A1 as a candidate tidal fragment near A+C with flat rotation, low velocity dispersion, and no detected ordered motion like F.

quotes.quote: For the first time, regions A1 and A2 , to the east of complex A+C, are seen in great detail and depth.

Fig. 2 shows the velocity gradients of the objects A1 , A2 , E and F, situated around the pair A+C (see Fig 1a) and which have been thought to be candidate tidal dwarf galaxies (Hunsberger et al. 1996, Iglesias-P aramo V i lchez 2001 and Richer et al. 2003). The curves are not corrected for inclination and the central position and velocity were chosen such that the curves were as symmetric as possible, with both sides matching, when possible. The object with the highest velocity gradient is E, with ordered velocities which range from 3950 km s ^ 1 to 4000 km s ^ 1 . Object A2 also shows ordered motion, with velocities going from 4125 to 4175 km s ^ 1 . Surprisingly, object F, thought to be the best tidal dwarf galaxy candidate in the group, shows a completely flat rotation curve, as does also the smaller object to the northeast of A+C, fragment A1 . In addition, A1 and F have low gaseous velocity dispersions.

There is also the possibility that regions A1 and F present no velocity gradient due to their rotation pattern be along the line of sight. Although this could be a possibility for the smaller and rounder region A1 , it is less likely the case for region F, given its elongated morphology.

sources.summary: The paper identifies arc-A as a very large, bright lensed galaxy behind SDSS J120923.7+264047, measuring z=1.018 and using its radius to estimate the lens mass.

quotes.quote: The most prominent arc in this system, labeled as A1--A4 in Fig. SDSS1209R , is very large and bright. This arc is located about 11'' East of the brightest cluster galaxy, and its length is 21'' . The spectrum of the arc, presented in Fig. GiantArcSpec , shows a single strong emission line and several absorption features, at a probable redshift of z=1.018 .

Therefore, the relatively small Einstein radius of arc-A implies that it is at relatively low redshift. Its redshift, which places it in the background of the cluster, along with its unique appearance, confirms the nature of this arc as a lensed galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a1 as a newly detected CO-rich overdensity member west of the FRII, close to a0 and a2, with a derived star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: Gas-rich members and global properties of the system We detected the FRII core ( a0 ) and three new members of the large-scale structure ( a1 , a2 , and a3 ). Three galaxies ( a0 , a1, and a2 ) lie within 80 kpc from each other, while a3 is located at 650 kpc from the FRII core. Interestingly, a1 , a2, and a3 are all distributed westward of the FRII. Exploiting the Schmidt-Kennicutt relation between the SFR surface density and the gas surface density _ SFR = 2.5 10^ 4 _ gas ^ 1.4 , we derived the SFRs for a1 , a2, and a3 .

sources.summary: The paper describes A01 as an edge-on disk AGN host where rotation strongly affects stellar velocity dispersion, producing the largest discrepancy between spatially resolved and single-aperture measurements.

quotes.quote: Most targets are close to face-on while one target (A01) is an edge-on galaxy.
In fact, we find that the edge-on galaxy, A01 showed the largest discrepancy between the spatially resolved SVD and the single-aperture based SVD.

This effect is most strikingly detected in A01 (see also A05), which is an edge-on disk galaxy with high rotation velocity while SVD is much weaker (below 100 \ even at the center). At the largest integration size of A01, the rotation-added SVD is 133.2 \ while the rotation-free SVD is 108.8 .

sources.summary: The paper reports J0205-55:A1 as a newly resolved, outside-SINGG-field galaxy, identifies it with ESO153-G020, and describes it as an SBab/Seyfert 2 system with outflow indications.

quotes.quote: With the ATCA we resolved the \ content of five galaxies: S1, S2, S3, S4 and one new galaxy (A1) which is not covered in the SINGG field of view.

J0205-55:A1 (ESO153-G020) is an SBab galaxy with a stellar mass of 10 ^ 10.59 M _ . Schmitt2003 classifies ESO153-G020 as Seyfert 2 galaxy, and Soto2019 finds indications for gas outflows at 400 km s ^ 2 .
J0209-10:S1 and J0205-55:A1 are classified as Seyfert 2 galaxies Carvalho1999, Schmitt2003 , and J2318-42a:S1 as Seyfert 1 Veron2006 .

sources.summary: A1 is a sub-mm/radio member of the assembling future BCG, strongly detected in CO and 870 m emission, merging in the cluster center, and highly star-forming.

quotes.quote: The assembling BCG: The two galaxies B1 and H5, detected in NIR and radio continuum, with a third (A1) detected in sub-mm and radio continuum, are expected to contribute to the formation of the future BCG of . coogan18 present a faint detection of the CO [ 4-3 ] line for B1 (3.7 ) and a 870 m continuum flux of 346 69 Jy whereas H5 has only been marginally detected ( 2.5 ) at 870 m. Conversely for A1, the CO [ 4-3 ] line has been detected at 11.9 and its 870 m continuum flux has been found to be 1370 140 Jy. Regarding the dynamics, both coogan18 and strazzullo18 conclude that B1, H5 and A1 along with H4; strazzullo18 are undergoing a merger at the cluster center. Moreover, B1 and H5 (along with H4) appear to be likely suppressed/quiescent galaxies lower main-sequence galaxies; sargent14 while A1 has a high SFR of 177.5 19 M _ yr ^ 1 , above what is expected from a main sequence galaxy Fig. 8 in coogan18 .

gmw_00004287.name: a2
gmw_00004287.overview: The active galaxy a2 is located at the center of Complex A [arXiv:2008.12129] and is classified as a candidate tidal dwarf galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0407525]. It exhibits ordered motion and counterrotates with respect to its immediate neighbor, galaxy A [arXiv:astro-ph/0407525].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ADF22.A2 as a near-infrared-dark, massive star-forming submillimeter galaxy at z=3.9913 with substantial molecular gas and properties expected of quiescent-galaxy progenitors.

quotes.quote: The detection of [ C i ] (1-0) and CO(4-3) emission lines determines the precise redshift of the galaxy, ADF22.A2, to be z=3.9913 0.0008 . On the basis of a multi-wavelength analysis, ADF22.A2 is found to be a massive, star-forming galaxy with a stellar mass of M_*=1.1_ 0.6 ^ +1.3 10^ 11 M_ and SFR =430_ 150 ^ +230 M_ yr ^ 1 . The molecular gas mass was derived to be M (H_2)^ CI =(5.9 1.5) 10^ 10 M_ , indicating a gas fraction of 35 , and the ratios of L_ CI (1-0) /L_ IR and L_ CI (1-0) /L_ CO(4-3) suggest that the nature of the interstellar medium in ADF22.A2 is in accordance with those of other bright submillimeter galaxies. The properties of ADF22.A2, including the redshift, star-formation rate, stellar mass, and depletion time scale ( _ dep 0.1-0.2 Gyr), also suggest that ADF22.A2 has the characteristics expected for the progenitors of quiescent galaxies at z 3 .

Our target, ADF22.A2, was originally discovered through our ALMA deep survey in the SSA22 field. This field represents a significant proto-cluster at z 3.1 (e.g., 1998ApJ...492..428S ; 2004AJ....128.2073H ; 2012AJ....143...79Y ), and the ALMA mosaic was designed to observe a 6^ 3^ region located at the proto-cluster core at 1.1 mm (ALMA deep field in SSA22 or ADF22; 2015ApJ...815L...8U ; 2017ApJ...835...98U ; 2018PASJ...70...65U ; 2019Sci...366...97U ; 2017PASJ...69...45H ). ADF22.A2 is the second brightest source in a 2^ 3^ subregion among ADF22 (ADF22A) with a flux density of S_ 1.1 mm =2.02 0.02 mJy ( 2017ApJ...835...98U ). ADF22.A2 was detected at 3.6 m and 4.5 m, while it was completely invisible even at the Ks band or shorter wavelengths (Table. table:photometry ).

sources.summary: The Dewdrop is a z=0.5798 strong galaxy-galaxy lens that produces two pairs of arcs from two sources and has [O II] source emission at z=0.9818.

quotes.quote: The first, (the ``Dewdrop''; z_ lens =0.5798 ), lenses two distinct extended sources into two pairs of arcs ( z_ source =0.9818 by nebular [ O II ] emission), while the second, (the ``Anchor''; z_ lens =0.4625 ), produces a single pair of arcs (source redshift not yet known). Four less convincing arc/counter-arc and two-image lens candidates are also found and presented for completeness. All three definite lenses are fit reasonably well by simple singular isothermal ellipsoid models including external shear, giving ^2_ values close to unity.

The Dewdrop lens at z_ lens =0.5798 lenses two distinct sources into two pairs of arcs. The Keck/DEIMOS spectrum of the system reveals anomalous [ O II ] nebular emission at z_ source =0.9818 (Fig.  fig:dewspec ). The sources in the Dewdrop system are part of a remarkable irregular and loose association of star formation knots and diffuse emitting material that extends over more than 10 arcsec, or more than 80 kpc comoving in size.

What seems surprising is that even though both the Dewdrop and Anchor lenses are in under dense environments locally, they still require relatively large shear contributions to produce good fits. In all three cases, we also note the large discrepancy between the modeled and LOS-predicted shear values. To explore this, we ran lens models with external shear and orientation restricted to the predicted values , and then examining the resulting models, and particularly the fit _ ^2 . All three new models require lenses with much higher ellipticity than the light suggests, though in the Dewdrop and the Anchor the formal _ ^2 remains plausible given the constraints, _ ^2=1.02 and 1.04 (or underfit by 1- and 2- ), respectively.

sources.summary: The paper identifies LAE A2 as a faint z=2.7713 Ly-alpha emitter 161 kpc from the QSO sightline and paired in redshift space with LAE A1 near Complex A.

quotes.quote: There are three foreground LAEs at d<300 kpc from the QSO sightline. Two of these objects, LAE A1 at peak redshift z_ peak = 2.7659 and LAE A2 at z_ peak = 2.7713 , are closely situated in redshift space with their Ly emission peaks separated by merely v 400 . In addition, both LAEs are situated on opposite sides of the QSO at a projected separation of 248 kpc, with the QSO sightline probing the LAE A1 at d=87 kpc and LAE A2 at d=161 kpc (top-left panel of Figure 3). A third emitter, LAE B1, occurs at z_ peak =2.7483 , and is probed by the background QSO at d=187 kpc (top-left panel of Figure 4).

The observed Ly line fluxes of the three LAEs are F_ Ly = (2.7 0.4) 10^ 18 , (3.3 0.6) 10^ 18 , and (3.5 0.5) 10^ 18 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 for LAEs A1, A2, and B1 (R08). These observed line fluxes correspond to Ly luminosities of L_ Ly =(1.7-2.2) 10^ 41 erg s^ 1 . The inferred Ly luminosities of the three LAEs are a factor of 20-30 lower than the characteristic luminosity of the LAE luminosity function at z 3 (e.g., Cassata \ 2011; Konno \ 2016; Drake \ 2017), which indicate that these LAEs are intrinsically faint compared to the general LAE population at the same epoch. This conclusion would stand even if the observations were subject to significant slit losses of 50 (e.g., R08; Cassata \ 2011).
Complex A is found near the redshifts of LAE A1 and LAE A2 (see absorption panels in the bottom of Figure 3).

sources.summary: The paper treats A2 as a candidate tidal dwarf near A+C, finding ordered motion, counterrotation relative to galaxy A, and likely fallback onto the merger complex.

quotes.quote: Our main findings are: (1) double kinematic components are present throughout the main body of A+C, which strongly suggests that this complex is an ongoing merger (2) regions A2 and E, to the east and south of complex A+C, present rotation patterns with velocity amplitudes of 25 km s^ 1 and they counterrotate with respect to A+C, (3) region F, which was previously thought to be the best example of a tidal dwarf galaxy in HCG 31, presents no rotation and negligible internal velocity dispersion, as is also the case for region A1 .

Fig. 2 shows the velocity gradients of the objects A1 , A2 , E and F, situated around the pair A+C (see Fig 1a) and which have been thought to be candidate tidal dwarf galaxies (Hunsberger et al. 1996, Iglesias-P aramo V i lchez 2001 and Richer et al. 2003). The curves are not corrected for inclination and the central position and velocity were chosen such that the curves were as symmetric as possible, with both sides matching, when possible. The object with the highest velocity gradient is E, with ordered velocities which range from 3950 km s ^ 1 to 4000 km s ^ 1 . Object A2 also shows ordered motion, with velocities going from 4125 to 4175 km s ^ 1 . Surprisingly, object F, thought to be the best tidal dwarf galaxy candidate in the group, shows a completely flat rotation curve, as does also the smaller object to the northeast of A+C, fragment A1 . In addition, A1 and F have low gaseous velocity dispersions.

As shown in Fig. 2, we detect ordered motions only for objects A2 and E and not for A1 and F. It might be suspected that the internal velocity motions measured in A2 and E could be due to streaming motions in incipient tidal tails in formation (see Fig. 1a). This is, however, not the case because these objects are counterrotating with respect to the main body of A+C. The discontinuity of the isovelocities can be clearly seen from Fig. 1c: the velocities go from high (northeast) to low (southwest) in A+C, towards object E. Then, along the body of E they go in the opposite sense. Similarly, for object A2 , it presents counterrotation with respect to its immediate neighbor to the west: galaxy A.

sources.summary: The paper describes AC114-A2 as a lensed z~1.9 high-metallicity starburst galaxy with substantial extinction, high nitrogen abundance, and a higher mass-to-light ratio than typical LBGs.

quotes.quote: In the present case, we targeted the two gravitationally amplified sources AC114-S2 and AC114-A2, both of them at redshift z 1.9 , together with the offset star. The redshifts measured for S2 and A2 are 1.867 and 1.869 respectively. For our adopted cosmology, the SFRs deduced from the \ luminosities are 30.3 and 15.0 \ for AC114-S2 and AC114-A2, respectively.

In contrast, A2 is a metal-rich galaxy ( \ 8.94 ; Z 1.3 ) with a high N/O abundance ratio (N/O 0.42 ), similar to those derived in the most metal-rich massive SBNGs. A2 seems on the contrary much more evolved. The location of this galaxy in the N/O versus O/H diagram indicates a relatively long star formation history with numerous powerful and extended starbursts.

sources.summary: A2 is presented as a z=1.87 lensed galaxy with measured optical emission lines, detected nitrogen emission, high metallicity and N/O, and evidence for an evolved star-formation history.

quotes.quote: Examples of YHK bands ISAAC spectra are shown in Fig. fig:spectra for the z=1.87 lensed galaxies S2 and A2 located behind the massive cluster AC 114 ( z=0.31 ). Relatively long integration times (typically 3-5 hours per band) are mandatory to get high enough S/N ratios. The brightest rest-frame optical emission lines ( , , and ) are clearly detected and measured in these spectra, as well as in the spectrum of the giant blue arc ( z=2.24 , hereafter Cl2244-gA) located behind the lensing cluster Cl 2244-02 ( z=0.33 ).

\ emission-line is not seen in these spectra as it falls at the blue edge of the H band. The \ is detected only in the spectrum of A2; an upper limit for the flux of this line has been derived for A2 and Cl2244-gA. Measuring this nitrogen line is very important to break the degeneracy between the oxygen abundance O/H and the strong emission-line intensity in order to get an accurate value of the metallicity.

In contrast, A2 is a metal-rich galaxy (Z 1.9 ) with a high N/O abundance ratio, similar to those derived in the most metal-rich massive SBNGs. The position of MS 1512-cB58 and CL2244-gA is intermediate between these two extremes showing abundance ratios typical of low-mass SBNGs and intermediate-redshift galaxies (see also Lemoine-Busserolle et al. 2003, 2004). A natural explanation for the variation of N/O at constant metallicity is the time delay between the release of oxygen and that of nitrogen into the ISM (e.g. Contini et al. 2002, and references therein), while maintaining a universal IMF and standard stellar nucleosynthesis. The ``delayed-release'' model assumes that star formation is an intermittent process in galaxies and predicts that the dispersion in N/O is due to the delayed release of nitrogen produced in low-mass longer-lived stars, compared to oxygen produced in massive, short-lived stars. Following this hypothesis and new chemical evolution models (Mouhcine Contini 2002), we might interpret the location of S2 and A2 in the N/O versus O/H diagram in terms of star formation history and evolutionary stage of these galaxies. The low O/H and N/O abundance ratios found in S2 might indicate a relatively young age for this object which experienced two or three bursts of star formation at most in the recent past. A2 seems on the contrary much more evolved. The location of this galaxy in the N/O versus O/H diagram indicates a relatively long star formation history with numerous powerful and extended starbursts.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a2 as an active galaxy in Complex A, infers strong X-ray obscuration, estimates a 3.2e7 solar-mass black hole accreting at Eddington ratio 0.51, and reports spatially correlated radio emission.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, in two of the analyzed sources, a2 at the center of Complex A and b1 in Complex B, the H emission line is broad (FWHM > 1500 km/s), indicating galaxies with active nuclei (AGN).

For a2 , from the bolometric luminosity, as derived from L(5100 ) (i.e. log(L_ bol / [ erg/s ] ) 45.4 ), and by applying k_ bol [ 2-10keV ] 18.96 Duras20 , we expect an intrinsic luminosity L_ [ 2-10keV ] 10^ 44 erg/s . Such value is more than 1dex higher compared to the derived X-ray luminosity upper limit, thus suggesting a high level of X-ray obscuration ( log(N_H/cm^2)>23.8 ). From the broad ( FWHM 1900 km/s ) H line, we estimated that the BH of a2 has a mass of M_ BH =3.2 10^7 M_ , and accretes at _ Edd 0.51 . While no emission has been found in ALMA corresponding to a2 , JVLA 1.5 GHz extended radio emission has been detected spatially correlated to it.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a2 as a newly detected CO-rich overdensity member west of the FRII, close to a0 and a1, with a derived star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: Gas-rich members and global properties of the system We detected the FRII core ( a0 ) and three new members of the large-scale structure ( a1 , a2 , and a3 ). Three galaxies ( a0 , a1, and a2 ) lie within 80 kpc from each other, while a3 is located at 650 kpc from the FRII core. Interestingly, a1 , a2, and a3 are all distributed westward of the FRII. Exploiting the Schmidt-Kennicutt relation between the SFR surface density and the gas surface density _ SFR = 2.5 10^ 4 _ gas ^ 1.4 , we derived the SFRs for a1 , a2, and a3 .

sources.summary: The paper notes A02 has problematic continuum absorption, gas motions inconsistent with stellar kinematics suggesting outflows, but little rotational-broadening bias because the galaxy is close to face-on.

quotes.quote: A couple of targets, e.g., A02, A03, and A09 show an absorption feature around , suggesting that the stellar continuum fit was not satisfactory. For these cases, we needed to add a negative Gaussian profile to correct for the absorption feature.
For example, A02, A04, A05, A06, A07, and A08 show a radial velocity profile of gas, which is inconsistent with that of stars, suggesting that that ionized gas is under the effect of outflows.
In contrast, A02 does not show such a strong difference since the galaxy is close to face-on and the projected rotation velocity is relatively low (see Figure kinematics ).

sources.summary: The paper describes E+A 2 as an E+A galaxy with disk-like photometric structure, a red core, and the largest angular-momentum proxy in the sample.

quotes.quote: The exception is the isophotal profile of E+A 2 (and to a lesser extent E+A 5) which appears more typical of a disk i.e. a strong downward concavity in the surface brightness profile.
A red core is evident in 4 of the 9 galaxies (E+A 2, E+A 7, E+A 8, E+A 10).
E+A 2 has a large _ R value in comparision to the other galaxies in the sample and is also the galaxy which showed the strongest evidence of a substantial disk (see Figures fig:images and fig:iso ).

gmw_00004288.name: a3
gmw_00004288.overview: The galaxy a3 is a gas-rich system located 650 kpc west of an FRII radio galaxy core [arXiv:2008.13665]. It is associated with a blend of two optical components [arXiv:2112.13779, arXiv:2008.13665] and has been previously classified as a type 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2009.11498].

sources.summary: The paper reidentifies SD850 COSMOS T1 A03 with an ALMA/radio counterpart, lowers its high-z index, and finds two close optical components inconsistent with z>5.

quotes.quote: For this SPIRE dropout, the ALMA data has identified its counterpart at 3 .0 from the SCUBA2 centroid. There is a prominent 3 GHz source at this location, which was noted in YMHF20 but was discarded because the separation is larger than the adopted criterion. With the new identification, this 3 GHz source is in fact the radio counterpart; the revised Hi z Idx is reduced to 0.22 and no longer satisfies the high- z criterion of Hi z Idx(850) >0.5 .

The source is invisible in I_ 814 . Unfortunately, it does not have H_ 160 coverage. In the HSC images, the source seems to have two very close components that cannot be separated for photometry. However, one can still tell that the ALMA position is closer to the northern component, which is also the fainter one among the two in the HSC images. Both components are visible in , and this rules out the possibility that this SPIRE dropout could be at z>5 .

sources.summary: The Anchor is a z=0.4625 strong lens producing a pair of arcs; models require external shear despite its locally underdense environment.

quotes.quote: The first, (the ``Dewdrop''; z_ lens =0.5798 ), lenses two distinct extended sources into two pairs of arcs ( z_ source =0.9818 by nebular [ O II ] emission), while the second, (the ``Anchor''; z_ lens =0.4625 ), produces a single pair of arcs (source redshift not yet known). Four less convincing arc/counter-arc and two-image lens candidates are also found and presented for completeness. All three definite lenses are fit reasonably well by simple singular isothermal ellipsoid models including external shear, giving ^2_ values close to unity.

The Anchor system exhibits a pair of arcs created by a lens at a redshift of z_ lens =0.4625 . The best-fitting lens model requires a significant external shear contribution (see Table  tab:lenses ), as might be expected from the position and shape of the counter-image to the main arc.

What seems surprising is that even though both the Dewdrop and Anchor lenses are in under dense environments locally, they still require relatively large shear contributions to produce good fits. In all three cases, we also note the large discrepancy between the modeled and LOS-predicted shear values. To explore this, we ran lens models with external shear and orientation restricted to the predicted values , and then examining the resulting models, and particularly the fit _ ^2 . All three new models require lenses with much higher ellipticity than the light suggests, though in the Dewdrop and the Anchor the formal _ ^2 remains plausible given the constraints, _ ^2=1.02 and 1.04 (or underfit by 1- and 2- ), respectively.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a3 as a newly detected CO-rich member west of the FRII, 650 kpc from its core, with the highest gas mass and SFR after a0.

quotes.quote: Gas-rich members and global properties of the system We detected the FRII core ( a0 ) and three new members of the large-scale structure ( a1 , a2 , and a3 ). Three galaxies ( a0 , a1, and a2 ) lie within 80 kpc from each other, while a3 is located at 650 kpc from the FRII core. Interestingly, a1 , a2, and a3 are all distributed westward of the FRII. Exploiting the Schmidt-Kennicutt relation between the SFR surface density and the gas surface density _ SFR = 2.5 10^ 4 _ gas ^ 1.4 , we derived the SFRs for a1 , a2, and a3 . It is interesting to note that a3 , which features the highest gas mass and SFR after a0 , is associated with a blend of two optical sources (HST image, Fig.

sources.summary: The paper treats A03 as the highest-redshift target, requiring alternate stellar templates because only the G-band is useful, with continuum-fit absorption issues and prior type 1 AGN classification.

quotes.quote: For one galaxy (A03), we used the INDO-US template spectra Valdes+04 since A03 is the highest redshift target among the sample and due to the redshift the promising stellar absorption feature is the G-band.

A couple of targets, e.g., A02, A03, and A09 show an absorption feature around , suggesting that the stellar continuum fit was not satisfactory. For these cases, we needed to add a negative Gaussian profile to correct for the absorption feature.
Six objects, namely, A01, A03, A04, A07, A08, A10 were previously classified as type 1 AGN by 2006A&A...455..773V . The classification is presumably based on the presence of a broad \ component.

gmw_00004289.name: Tol 0440-381
gmw_00004289.overview: Tol 0440-381 is a nearby candidate Lyman-continuum emitting H II galaxy [arXiv:2201.03138] with a stellar mass of approximately 10^10 solar masses [arXiv:2201.03138]. It exhibits extended X-ray emission and contains a luminous X-ray source that brightened by at least a factor of four over 3.8 days [arXiv:2201.03138].

sources.summary: The paper treats Tol0440-381 as a known LyC emitter whose low redshift complicates Lyman-series fitting, requiring nonparametric H i treatment and O i 1302 measurements.

quotes.quote: We note that Tol0440-381 and Mrk54 have much lower redshifts than the rest of the sample. Consequently there are more Milky Way absorption lines near the Lyman series. This high density of Milky Way Lyman series absorption lines means that we cannot accurately fit the Lyman series with Voigt profiles. Rather, we used nonparametric fits to describe the H i properties (see Sect. method:depth ). The H i column densities are affected by large uncertainties because the low S/N and insufficient resolution of the spectra do not allow a reliable estimate of \ given the saturation of the H i absorption lines (see Sect. simcold ).

Except for Tol0440-381 and Mrk54, where we did not fit the absorption lines with the method in Sect. fitting , we computed the final C_f ( H i ) as the weighted mean of the C_f depth and the fitted C_f values (column 8 in Table table:hiresult ).
However, O i is undetected for J0925+1409 (see Fig. fig:J0925 ), and we used O i 1302 \ for Tol0440-381 and Mrk54.

sources.summary: The paper presents Tol 0440-381 as a nearby candidate LyC-emitting H II galaxy with extended Chandra X-ray emission and a rapidly brightening luminous X-ray source.

quotes.quote: Chandra observations of the nearby, candidate Lyman-continuum (LyC) emitting galaxy Tol 0440-381 show brightening of an X-ray source by at least a factor of 4 to a luminosity of 1.6 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 over 3.8 days. Tol 0440-381 (hereafter Tol0440) is an H ii galaxy with possible Wolf Rayet features and a stellar mass of 10^ 10 M_ Leitet2013 . Hence, Tol0440 is a candidate LyC emitter. We obtained X-ray observations of Tol0440 with Chandra . There is extended X-ray emission coincident with Tol0440 in both observations.

gmw_00004290.name: Tol 1247-232
gmw_00004290.overview: Tol 1247-232 is a nearby starburst galaxy containing a young stellar cluster [arXiv:1704.05943, arXiv:1708.05074] and a variable, accreting X-ray source that is likely a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1708.05074, arXiv:1802.01953]. It is one of the few galaxies known to leak ionizing Lyman-continuum radiation [arXiv:1704.05943, arXiv:2201.03138].

sources.summary: The paper confirms LyC escape from Tololo 1247-232 with a low escape fraction, observes the central clusters, and constrains its atomic gas reservoir and H I halo.

quotes.quote: Tololo 1247-232 is among those very few galaxies with recently reported leakage. We performed intermediate resolution ultraviolet (UV) spectroscopy with the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (COS) onboard the Hubble Space Telescope and confirm ionizing radiation emerging from Tololo 1247-232. Adopting an improved data reduction procedure, we find that LyC escapes from the central stellar clusters, with an escape fraction of 1.5 0.5 only, i.e. the lowest value reported for the galaxy so far.

We also performed VLA imaging. The hydrogen hyperfine transition was not detected, but a deep upper limit atomic gas mass of 10^9 M_ could be derived. The upper limit gas fraction defined as M_ H I M_* is only 20 . Evidence is found that the H I gas halo is relatively small compared to other Lyman Alpha emitters.

sources.summary: The paper resolves Tol 1247-232 in X-rays, finding luminous variable accretion-powered emission and discussing its young starburst and possible role in LyC escape.

quotes.quote: Chandra observations of the nearby, Lyman-continuum (LyC) emitting galaxy Tol 1247-232 resolve the X-ray emission and show that it is dominated by a point-like source with a hard spectrum ( = 1.6 0.5 ) and a high luminosity ( (9 2) 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 ). Comparison with an earlier XMM-Newton observation shows flux variation of a factor of 2. Hence the X-ray emission likely arises from an accreting X-ray source: a low-luminosity AGN or one or a few X-ray binaries.

The Chandra images, Fig. xray_images , show that Tol1247 produces soft emission spread across the central part of the galaxy and point-like emission with a luminosity of (9 2) 10^ 40 erg s^ 1 and a hard spectrum with a photon index of 1.56 0.46 . The variability is strong evidence for the presence of at least one highly luminous accreting source in Tol1247. Rosa2007 concluded that Tol1247 contains a single, young star burst with an age of less than 4 Myr, based on the galaxy's high H equivalent width, high Oiii to H ratio, shallow radio spectral index, and low ratio of 1.4 GHz versus H flux.

sources.summary: The paper describes Tol 1247-232 as a candidate LyC emitter and known X-ray galaxy whose accreting X-ray source could explain its high ionization parameter.

quotes.quote: LyC emission was first reported from Tol 1247-232 (hereafter Tol1247) with an escape fraction of 2.4^ +0.9 _ 0.8 per cent using FUSE data Leitet2013 . Hence, Tol1247 is a candidate LyC emitter. Haro 11 and Tol1247 are known X-ray emitters. X-ray emission from Tol 1247-232 was first detected at L_X = 2.3 10^ 41 erg s^ 1 with XMM-Newton which lacks the spatial resolution needed to the resolve the galaxy Rosa2009 . Indeed, Micheva2018 find an extremely high ionization parameter in Tol1247 that could be explained by the accreting X-ray source.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Tol 1247-232 as a LyC-emitting galaxy with an X-ray-variable low-luminosity AGN, concluding that its ionizing radiation likely also includes surrounding stellar emission.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, Kaaret et al. (2017) find that the Lyman continuum emitting galaxy Tol 1247-232 ( f_ esc,rel =21.6 , Leitherer et al. 2016) has been detected in X-ray as a point-like source by Chandra, and it shows also X-ray variability by a factor of 2 in few years, thus being probably a low-luminosity AGN ( Lx 10^ 41 erg/s ) at z=0.048 .

The total UV absolute magnitudes of Tol 1247-232 and Haro 11 are 20/-21 , which translate into effective magnitudes of 15/-16 at 900 rest frame, given the observed values for their LyC escape fraction. Starting from the X-ray luminosity of Lx 10^ 41 erg/s measured by Kaaret et al. (2017) for their central sources, and assuming that the optical luminosities are scaling as Lx L_ UV ^ 0.6 (Lusso Risaliti 2016), we derive an absolute magnitude of M_ 1450 12 for the central AGNs in Tol 1247-232 and Haro 11. This implies that the ionizing radiation by these two galaxies cannot be entirely produced by the central engines, even assuming an escape fraction of 100 for the central AGN, but it is possibly emitted also by the surrounding stars, once the AGN has cleaned the surrounding ISM.

sources.summary: The paper revisits Tol 1247-232 LyC measurements, finding lower reanalyzed flux and treating shadow-only apparent detections as upper limits.

quotes.quote: Correspondingly, Haro 11, J0921 + 4509, and Tol 1247 381 are all fairly extended and have / ratios above 2.5, while the more compact galaxies have smaller / ratios.

In fact, Puschnig reanalyze the Tol 1247-232 data and find similarly decreased of 1.5 0.5 , after discovering that the CALCOS 2.20d pipeline fails at low/negative flux levels when superdark frames are included.
Due to possible residual contamination from geocoronal Lyman series and scattered light, we regard the apparent detections of Tol 0440-381 and Tol 1247-232 in only the shadow data as upper limits.

gmw_00004291.name: 4C +74.13
gmw_00004291.overview: 4C +74.13 is a radio and elliptical galaxy characterized as a large-core galaxy [arXiv:1910.10240]. High-resolution imaging of the galaxy has been used to model its outer light profile and determine its core-Sersic break radius [arXiv:1910.10240].

sources.summary: The paper includes 4C +74.13 as a large-core galaxy, discusses its archival imaging limits, steeper core profile, and revised core-Sersic break radius.

quotes.quote: The case of the BCG IC 1101 is published in 2017MNRAS.471.2321D and the remaining 12 galaxies consist of 9 galaxies with r_ > 0.5 kpc drawn from the 2007ApJ...664..226L , 4C +74.13 r_ = 1.54 kpc 2009ApJ...698..594M , the BCG of Abell A2261 r_ = 3.95 kpc 2012ApJ...756..159P and the giant cD galaxy NGC 4486 ( 2011MNRAS.415.2158R , R_ b 0.5 kpc; 2013AJ....146..160R , R_ b 0.7 kpc). Basic data for the full sample of 13 galaxies are listed in Table  Table1 . The high-resolution ACS WFC images were sufficiently extended in radius to robustly determine the shapes of the outer parts of the light profiles for the two farthest (D > 925 Mpc) galaxies in our sample (4C +74.13 and A2261-BCG), Fig.  Fig1 .

While the remaining two galaxies NGC 4486 and 4C +74.13 have > 0.15 , our Gaussian PSF-convolved models give excellent fits to the galaxies' HST profiles (Appendix  AppA ). Our core-S ersic break radii are ( 25 ) larger than the cusp radii reported for four galaxies in common with 2007ApJ...664..226L , NGC 4874 , NGC 4889 , NGC 6166 and A2147-BCG , and for 4C +74.13 2009ApJ...698..594M .

gmw_00004292.name: A2261-BCG
gmw_00004292.overview: A2261-BCG is a distant brightest cluster galaxy and core elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1910.10240, arXiv:1611.06573]. It features an exceptionally large depleted core [arXiv:1610.00801, arXiv:1910.10240] and contains multiple nuclei located within this core region [arXiv:1611.06573].

sources.summary: The paper confirms A2261-BCG has an exceptionally large depleted core, estimates its luminosity deficit and black-hole implications, and argues that a stalled massive perturber may explain the core.

quotes.quote: From the fit to A2261-BCG we obtained a large break radius R_ b,cS = 3.63 kpc, while for SDSS-H5 we obtained a more ``modest'' R_ b,cS = 0.55 kpc. In Comparing our fit results with the literature we compare these core sizes with the results obtained in the studies of A2261-BCG postman and SDSS-H5 hyde , and we relate them to the typical values observed in early-type galaxies.

Our fit to A2261-BCG confirmed that the galaxy has a large depleted core, rather than just a shallow inner light profile. The luminosity deficit ( L_ def ) that we derived as the difference between the profile and the inward extrapolation of its part (see Mass deficits ) is 1.43 10 ^ 11 L_ .

Moreover, the extended wings of knot 3 might be confused with the underlying light of the galaxy; this may have reduced the object luminosity recovered by our fit. Given these considerations, knot 3 represents a plausible candidate perturber to explain the unusually large radial extent of the core in A2261-BCG. Knots 1 and 2 might have contributed to the excavation of the core (although to a lesser extent) through an analogous infall process.

sources.summary: The paper includes A2261-BCG as a distant large-core brightest cluster galaxy and cautions that its neighbor-density measures may be biased bright.

quotes.quote: The case of the BCG IC 1101 is published in 2017MNRAS.471.2321D and the remaining 12 galaxies consist of 9 galaxies with r_ > 0.5 kpc drawn from the 2007ApJ...664..226L , 4C +74.13 r_ = 1.54 kpc 2009ApJ...698..594M , the BCG of Abell A2261 r_ = 3.95 kpc 2012ApJ...756..159P and the giant cD galaxy NGC 4486 ( 2011MNRAS.415.2158R , R_ b 0.5 kpc; 2013AJ....146..160R , R_ b 0.7 kpc). Basic data for the full sample of 13 galaxies are listed in Table  Table1 . The high-resolution ACS WFC images were sufficiently extended in radius to robustly determine the shapes of the outer parts of the light profiles for the two farthest (D > 925 Mpc) galaxies in our sample (4C +74.13 and A2261-BCG), Fig.  Fig1 .

The caveat here is that galaxies with M_ B 19.5 mag may be too faint for detection at the distances of 4C +74.13 (D 925 Mpc), A2261-BCG (D 959 Mpc) and IC 1101 (D 363 Mpc), see Figs.  Fig10A , Fig10 and Appendix  AppB . Therefore, we caution that A2261-BCG and IC 1101 can be biased toward having brighter nearest neighbours and low _ 10 , compared to the other large-core galaxies with D 213 Mpc (see Appendix  AppB ).

sources.summary: The paper cites A2261-BCG as a core elliptical with multiple nuclei whose core and central SMBH mass make it relevant to stalled satellite SMBH interpretations.

quotes.quote: More recent observations suggest the presence of multiple nuclei located (in projection) within the core elliptical galaxy A2261-BCG 2016ApJ...829...81B . The estimated mass of the core and the central SMBH in A2261-BCG is M_ core M_ 2 10^ 10 . This implies that if a SMBH is present at the center of this galaxy, then such nuclei are residing well inside its influence radius.

gmw_00004293.name: G1
aliases_external: G1 (west) in the J2346+124 DLA host group
gmw_00004293.overview: G1 is an intermediately-inclined galaxy with a half-light radius of 6.6 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2206.12501]. Located at a redshift of 2.431, it is the brightest member of its compact group and exhibits both emission and interstellar medium absorption lines [arXiv:2206.12501].

sources.summary: G1 is the brightest member of the z=2.431 compact group, with emission and absorption lines, an L-star luminosity, intermediate inclination, and possible links to outflow or accretion signatures.

quotes.quote: G1 is located =2 7 directly east of the quasar sightline and is the brightest member of the group in all of the HST bands, KCWI, and in emission. It is the westernmost galaxy in the group, with no emitters on top of the quasar. A previous survey of the field did not survey far enough to cover G1 or the rest of the compact group (i.e. no emitting galaxies were located within 1 5 or 12.5 kpc of the quasar to SFR limits ranging SFR >4.4-11 M _ yr ^ 1 ; wang2015 ). G1 has both emission and ISM absorption lines, which are labelled in the KCWI spectrum of Fig. fig:galaxies (top). We measured a redshift using several ISM absorption lines covered in the KCWI data.
G1 is roughly an L^ galaxy with L_B/L_B^ =1.66 . It has an observed colour of F110W F160W =0.5 , which is comparable to the other galaxies in the group.

Using this method, galaxy G1 is best modelled with i=66^ and =17^ , classifying it as an intermediately-inclined galaxy with the quasar probing closest to the projected major axis and a half-light radius of R_ 1/2 =6.6 kpc (Table tab:HSTgalprops ).

gmw_00004294.name: G2
aliases_external: G2 (south) in the J2346+124 DLA host group
gmw_00004294.overview: G2 is a blue, sub-L-star group galaxy with a redshift of 2.4322 [arXiv:2206.12501]. It exhibits Ly-alpha emission with both red and blue peaks, and its projected major axis is probed by a nearby quasar sightline [arXiv:2206.12501].

sources.summary: G2 is a blue, sub-L-star group member southeast of the quasar, with Ly-alpha emission, measured redshift, star formation, and a major-axis sightline that may trace outflow or accretion.

quotes.quote: G2 is located =3 8 southeast of the quasar (Table tab:DLAgals ) and has both red and blue peaks in emission, but no identifiable ISM absorption lines due to the low continuum level in the KCWI data (Fig. fig:galaxies ). Its redshift is z_ G2 =2.4322 0.0008 , which was measured from the red emission peak following the methods described for G1 in Section sec:G1 (Table tab:KCWIgalprops ). The galaxy then has an impact parameter of D=30.6 kpc making it the third-nearest galaxy to the quasar. G2 is the bluest galaxy of the group with an observed F110W-F160W colour of 0.9 , which could either be a result of a higher star formation rate or simply less dust extinction than the other galaxies. The latter may be more correct since we observe both the red and blue peaks of where typically only a red peak is observed, and the blue peak has a similar strength as the red peak.

G2 is a sub- L^ galaxy with absolute magnitude M_B=-19.9 and luminosity L_B/L_B^ =0.12 (Table tab:HSTgalprops ) and a UV absolute magnitude M_ UV =-19.8 (Table tab:KCWIgalprops ). From the GIM2D modeling, we find that G2 is best modelled with a half-light radius of R_ 1/2 =2.9 kpc, an intermediate inclination, i=58^ , and with the quasar sightline probing its projected major axis, =14^ . Similar to G1, G2 may give rise to both metal-rich outflowing and metal-poor accretion signatures.

gmw_00004295.name: G3
aliases_external: G3 (east) in the J2346+124 DLA host group
gmw_00004295.overview: G3 is a sub-L* galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.428 [arXiv:2206.12501]. It exhibits a low star formation rate and a low star formation surface density that is insufficient to drive outflows [arXiv:2206.12501].

sources.summary: G3 is the farthest detected group galaxy from the quasar, a sub-L-star, low-SFR object with Ly-alpha emission, redshift z=2.428, and likely accretion rather than outflow signatures.

quotes.quote: Galaxy G3 is the furthest of the group galaxies from the quasar with =4 9 directly east of the quasar (Table tab:DLAgals ). It has a red peak, with a possible blue peak that is on the level of the noise. The red peak gives a corrected redshift of z_ G3 =2.428 0.001 following the methods described for G1 (Table tab:KCWIgalprops ), but there are no identifiable ISM absorption features in the spectrum due to the low continuum level (Fig. fig:galaxies ). G3 is then at an impact parameter of D=39.6 kpc and is a sub- L^ galaxy with M_ UV =-19.6 , M_B=-20.1 , and L_B/L_B^ =0.15 (Table tab:HSTgalprops ). It has the reddest observed F110W F160W colour out of the group (0.6) and a low SFR with SFR _ >0.7 M _ yr ^ 1 , measured using the sobral19 relation with only the red peak and f_ esc, LyC =0 ( SFR _ >0.8 for f_ esc, LyC =0.15 ).

The SFR _ FUV =1.4 M _ yr ^ 1 is also low, but could be as large as 5.9 M _ yr ^ 1 assuming more dust extinction. The SFR surface density of _ SFR =0.008 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 is too low to drive significant outflows, even assuming more dust extinction ( _ SFR =0.037 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 ). G3 is best modelled as an intermediately-inclined, i=30^ , galaxy with the quasar probing its projected major axis, =12^ , with a half-light radius of R_ 1/2 =7.2 kpc. Given its properties, G3 would likely give rise to accretion signatures in the quasar sightline, but would not have recently contributed outflowing material.

gmw_00004296.name: G4
aliases_external: G4 (north) in the J2346+124 DLA host group
gmw_00004296.overview: G4 is a star-forming group galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.4310 [arXiv:2206.12501]. It exhibits weak interstellar medium lines, lacks clear Lyman-alpha emission, and displays possible tidal features suggesting interactions with neighboring galaxies [arXiv:2206.12501].

sources.summary: G4 is a likely associated northern group galaxy with weak ISM lines, redshift z=2.4310, no clear Ly-alpha emission, possible tidal features, and low outflow potential.

quotes.quote: The fourth galaxy in this group, G4, is located =3 3 east and slightly north of the quasar sightline. It is not directly observed in the KCWI line map in Fig. fig:galspec and there is no obvious emission in its spectrum (Fig. fig:galaxies d), which suggests it is either not a emitter or is at a different redshift than this group. G4 is of similar size, luminosity, and observed colour ( F110W F160W =0.4 ) as the other three galaxies in the group so it is likely that it is also associated, and the F160W image appears to show it has tidal features in the directions of G3 and G1/G2. Close inspection of the galaxy's spectrum shows weak ISM lines, particularly in the doublet, which give an average redshift of z_ G4 =2.4310 0.0001 . We cannot reliably estimate a redshift from as was done for the other three galaxies since any potential emission is on the level of the noise in the spectrum (e.g. the peak at 20 in Fig. fig:galaxies (d)).

All four galaxies are star-forming, although G1 is more active than G2, G3, and G4 by at least an order of magnitude. Both G1 and G2 have star formation rate surface densities above the fiducial threshold to drive outflows if we assume the upper range of dust extinction, whereas G3 and G4 are lower by over an order of magnitude and likely not driving outflows.

gmw_00004297.name: SDSS J105816.19+544310.2
gmw_00004297.overview: SDSS J105816.19+544310.2 is a blue quasar located at a redshift of 0.479 [arXiv:2209.02164]. It is identified as a robust candidate for a true type-2 quasar due to its quasar-like blue continuum emissions and the apparent absence of broad Balmer and Mg II emission lines [arXiv:2209.02164].

sources.summary: The paper argues SDSS J105816.19+544310.2 is a robust true type-2 blue quasar, with quasar-like blue continuum but missing broad Balmer and Mg II emission lines.

quotes.quote: In the manuscript, the blue quasar SDSS J105816.19+544310.2 (= ) at redshift 0.479 is reported as so-far the best candidate of true type-2 quasar with disappearance of central BLRs.

Here, not similar as previous reported candidates for true type-2 AGN among narrow emission-line galaxies with weak AGN activities but strong stellar lights, the definite blue quasar can be well confirmed as a true type-2 quasar due to apparent quasar-shape blue continuum emissions but apparent loss of both the optical broad Balmer emission lines and the NUV broad Mg ii emission line. Based on different model functions and F-test statistical technique, after considering blue-shifted optical and UV Fe ii emissions, there are no apparent broad optical Balmer emission lines and/or broad NUV Mg ii line, and the confidence level is smaller than 1sigma to support broad optical and NUV emission lines. Moreover, assumed the Virialization assumption to broad line emission clouds, the re-constructed broad emission lines strongly indicate that the probable intrinsic broad emission lines, if there were, cannot be hidden or overwhelmed in the noises of SDSS spectrum of . Therefore, , so-far the best and robust candidate of true type-2 quasar, leads to the further clear conclusion on the very existence of true type-2 AGN.

gmw_00004298.name: XID-312
gmw_00004298.overview: XID-312 is a low-mass galaxy that hosts a candidate active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1305.3826]. The galaxy is reported to be X-ray variable, and its X-ray hardness ratio suggests absorption by neutral gas [arXiv:1305.3826].

sources.summary: The paper presents XID-312 as a low-mass X-ray AGN candidate with variability, possible broad H emission, absorption, disk-dominated morphology, and a black hole mass consistent with local scaling relations.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present three candidate AGNs in low-mass galaxies, selected to have a high ratio of X-ray to optical luminosity as detailed below. If confirmed, these galaxies would be among the lowest-mass host galaxies known see also barth2008,reinesetal2011 . Two of them (XID-476, XID-231) are detected in the shallower E-CDF-S coverage lehmer2005 and the third (XID-312) within the central 4 Ms area xue2011 with more than 100 counts in the full band (0.5-8 keV). In Figure  fig:optical , we show postage-stamp HST/ACS images of the three low-mass galaxies while indicating the X-ray centroid and an effective search radius for an optical counterpart that demonstrates the likelihood of these associations.
