sources.summary: The paper counts NGC 1106 as a bona fide Compton-thick AGN with consistent model fits, CT line-of-sight and torus columns, and reflection-dominated emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 1106 This candidate was marked as a CT-AGN, based on the data of \ and , claiming it to have log N _ H =24.25 _ 0.17 ^ +0.29 in cm ^ 2 . For our analysis, we have used \ observation (taken in 03/2019; 33 ks) and \ observations (taken in 02-2019 and 09-2020; total exposure 41 ks). The cross-calibration ratio between and detectors is 1.4 . This source is very well fitted (see Table Table:best-fit_NGC1106 and Figure fig:NGC1106 ). All three models show consistent results with each other.

The l.o.s. column density N _ H,l.o.s. =(2.83-5.73) 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 shows a CT column density, which validates the result of . The torus average column density is N _ H,tor =(1.34-7.98) 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 , in agreement with the CT nature of the torus. From the above analysis, this source can be counted as a bona fide CT-AGN.

gmw_00004507.name: ESO406-G004
gmw_00004507.overview: ESO406-G004 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:2305.07705]. Although previously considered a candidate Compton-thick active galactic nucleus, X-ray observations show it is mostly Compton-thin and exhibits large variability [arXiv:2305.07705].

sources.summary: The paper finds ESO406-G004 mostly Compton-thin, with low-count soft X-ray data, variable observations, and torus upper bounds that cross the Compton-thick threshold.

quotes.quote: ESO406-G004 This target was marked as a CT-AGN candidate based on the data of \ and , claiming to have log N _ H =24.74 _ 0.55 ^ +* in cm ^ 2 . For our analysis, we have a \ observation with very low exposure ( 5.1 ks) and only 25 spectral counts in soft X-rays. Furthermore, even though the two archival \ observations have a much higher exposure time (total 60 ks), the source count statistic is significantly lower than that of the other sources ( 747 net counts in 2016 and 206 net counts in 2020; see Table tabel:1 ). Due to such low spectral counts, we have used C-Statistics to fit the data after binning with 1 count/bin in , 10 counts/bin and 20 counts/bin on \ observations of 2020 and 2016 respectively. It is also noticeable that the observations were taken after large gaps ( 4 years), and the cross-calibration ratio between ACIS (of ) and FMPA (of ) detectors show large variability 1.3-1.7 .

This source is very well fitted by all the models. All the three models show consistent results with each other. The l.o.s. column density for this source N _ H,l.o.s. =(0.59-1.28) 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 shows a mostly Compton-Thin column density, contrary to the result of .

gmw_00004508.name: 2MASX J20145928+2523010
gmw_00004508.overview: 2MASX J20145928+2523010 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2305.07705, arXiv:2211.04478] that is optically very red [arXiv:2211.04478]. The source exhibits X-ray flux variability [arXiv:2305.07705] and was detected as a transient source in optical photometry [arXiv:2211.04478].

sources.summary: The paper finds 2MASX J20145928+2523010 to be Compton-thin rather than CT, with strong flux variability and intrinsic power-law domination over reflection.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J20145928+2523010 This candidate was also classified as a CT-AGN, based on the data of \ and , claiming to have log N _ H =24.42 _ 0.17 ^ +0.20 in cm ^ 2 . For our analysis, we have (taken in 12/2018) and (taken in 11/2017) spectra with excellent photon statistics in the 0.6-10 keV energy range (total spectral counts 9 k). Even in hard X-rays, the two archival \ observations (taken in 05/2017 and 04/2020) have a high exposure time and net spectral counts (total 3.6 k). It is worth noting, that for the joint \ and \ observation taken in 2017, we measure a cross-calibration ratio <1.4 . However, the flux of the 2018 \ and the 2020 \ observation is almost twice ( 1.93-2.15 ) that of the 2017 observation.

There is also a significant flux variability (factor of 0.77) between the \ and \ observations taken only 6 months apart. This source is very well fitted by all the models (see Table Table:best-fit_2MASXJ20145928+2523010 ). All the three models show consistent results with each other.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MASX J20145928+2523010 as a red Compton-thick AGN with a new broad emission feature, ZTF transient behavior, and declining BAT flux.

quotes.quote: 2MASX J20145928+2523010 2MASX J20145928+2523010 (BAT 1070) is a Compton-thick ( N_ H =10^ 24.2 cm ^ 2 ) AGN which is also very red ( g 2 mag) in optical imaging. Correspondingly, the signal-to-noise in the \ region of the optical spectra is poor. In 2017 August only narrow \ emission is detected, but in 2019 June a broad emission feature appears around _ rest =6510 , which we interpret as broad, albeit blueshifted, \ emission. BAT 1070 was detected as a transient source in ZTF photometry (ZTF19aawrrqy; AT 2019aagk; 2020TNSTR3485....1D ), with a 0.7 mag brightening in the band within 6 months in the second half of 2020 which has since faded.

gmw_00004509.name: IRAS 0738-2342
gmw_00004509.overview: IRAS 0738-2342 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy characterized by an extremely gas-poor environment [arXiv:1106.1676]. Spectral energy distribution fits indicate that its active galactic nucleus and starburst activity contribute roughly equally to its bolometric luminosity [arXiv:1106.1676].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IRAS 0738-2342 as the current sample's most extreme gas-poor ULIRG point and cites SED fits implying roughly equal AGN and starburst bolometric contributions.

quotes.quote: The most extreme point from the current sample is the source IRAS 0738-2342, while in the comb11 sample it is IRAS 19104+8436. Two addtional sources in the Northern sample, [ HB89 ] 1821+643 and F 00415-0737, are also discrepant but less extreme. While very little is currently known about F 00415-0737, ruiz10 have recently presented SED fits for IRAS 0738-2342 and [ HB89 ] 1821+643. They estimate relative AGN:Starburst contributions to the bolometric luminosity of about 50:50 and 80:20 for these two sources.

gmw_00004510.name: MRC 0200+015
gmw_00004510.overview: MRC 0200+015 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.229 [arXiv:1104.3608]. It is detected as an H-alpha emitter with extended emission spanning scales of 25 to 40 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1104.3608].

sources.summary: The paper targets the high-redshift radio galaxy MRC 0200+015 as a signpost of a z=2.229 overdensity, detecting it as an H-alpha emitter with extended emission.

quotes.quote: Our second target is a HzRG: MRC 0200+015. The number density of HzRGs at z= 2--5 is a few times 10^ 8 Mpc ^ 3 2007A&ARv..15...67M , and thus HzRGs are quite rare. There is growing evidence that a significant fraction of HzRGs reside in over-dense environments 2000A&A...358L...1K, 2000A&A...361L..25P, 2003Natur.425..264S, 2003ApJ...599...86S, 2004Natur.427...47M, 2006MNRAS.371..577K, 2007MNRAS.377.1717K, 2007A&A...461..823V, 2010arXiv1012.1869T, 2011arXiv1103.4364H .

Using NED, we identified a HzRG, MRC 0200+015 (or NVSS J020242+014910) at z=2.229 hereafter 0200+015, 1981MNRAS.194..693L, 1997A&A...326..505R, 1998AJ....115.1693C . This HzRG field was observed using H imaging by 2000A&A...362..509V . They imaged an area of just 6.37 arcmin ^2 down to a 3 flux limit of 1.0 10 ^ 16 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 for H emitters in redshift range of z= 2.19--2.26. Although they detected the HzRG as an H emitter, they did not identify any additional H emitter candidates around the HzRG.

In the 0200+015 field, our deeper, higher-resolution MOIRCS narrow-band imaging confirms that the H emission from three HAEs (including both the HzRG and the newly discovered narrow-line AGN) is significantly extended on scales of 25--40 kpc.

gmw_00004511.name: HAE17
gmw_00004511.overview: HAE17 is a high-redshift emission-line galaxy located in the 2QZ field [arXiv:1104.3608]. It is an extended H-alpha emitter with a spatial extent of 30 to 60 kiloparsecs, suggesting large-scale star formation or gas outflows interacting with the circumgalactic medium [arXiv:1104.3608].

sources.summary: The paper highlights HAE17 in the 2QZ field as the most extended H-alpha emitter, with possible Ly-alpha-blob-like luminosity and spatial extent.

quotes.quote: We find several examples of such extended HAEs in our target fields, including a striking example in the 2QZ field, suggesting that these are relatively common in high-density regions, with no clear examples in our control field or reported in the field survey of G08. The spatial extent of these systems, 30--60 kpc, suggests that star formation at z= 2.23 is occuring over regions in these galaxies comparable to the size of the largest galaxies at the present day or large-scale gas outflows are interacting with the surrounding circum galactic medium in overdense environments. Looking at the H luminosity of the most extended example, 6 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 for HAE17 in the 2QZ field, if we assume Ly /H = 8.75 case B and no dust extinction, e.g. 1992ApJ...387L..29M , then its Ly luminosity is expected to be 5 10 ^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , which would make its spatial extent and Ly luminosity comparable to those of giant Ly blobs 2011MNRAS.410L..13M .

gmw_00004512.name: J0011+1446
gmw_00004512.overview: J0011+1446 is a high-redshift radio source [arXiv:1701.06622] classified as a flat-spectrum source [arXiv:1701.06622]. It is compact on angular scales between 5 arcseconds and 5 milliarcseconds [arXiv:1609.00575] and is likely non-variable [arXiv:1609.00575].

sources.summary: The paper compares FIRST, NVSS, and EVN measurements of J0011+1446, finding nearby blended sources explain much of the flux mismatch and concluding the source is compact and likely non-variable.

quotes.quote: J0011+1446 has a FIRST and NVSS flux density of 24.3 1.2 and 35.8 1.5 mJy, respectively. Looking at the higher-resolution FIRST image, there is a second and third source that are 16.4 and 29.3 arcsec away from J0011+1446 with flux densities of 3.0 0.2 and 2.8 0.2 mJy, respectively. Since the NVSS beam size is 45 arcsec nvss , both of the nearby sources will blend with J0011+1446 in NVSS. However, the sum of the flux densities of these two sources is still less than the difference between the FIRST and NVSS flux densities. The remaining flux density difference could be explained by variability of J0011+1446 or of the two nearby sources.

From Table tbl:Flux density ratios , S_ pred /S_ FIRST nearly equals 1 for J0011+1446. Considering that the source is not variable, this indicates that J0011+1446 is compact on angular scales between 5 arcsec and 5 mas.

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0011+1446 as a flat-spectrum high-redshift radio source, discarding blended low-resolution catalog matches and noting possible resolved-out or variable VLBI flux.

quotes.quote: Flat-spectrum sources The following six sources all have flat spectra (they can be fitted by a single power law with 0.5< <0.5 ). J0011+1446 We matched J0011+1446 to sources in the 148 MHz TGSS, National Radio Astronomy Observatory (NRAO) VLA Sky Survey and 4.9 GHz GB6 catalogues. However, in the 1.4 GHz FIRST catalogue there are two sources that are 16.4 and 29.3 arcsec away from the J0011+1446 VLBI position. Since the flux density of these sources will blend with that of J0011+1446 in the lower resolution TGSS, 1.4 GHz NVSS and GB6 catalogues, we discarded these matches. The spectrum is shown in Fig. fig:J0011+1446 . Fitting a power law between the FIRST and 8.5 GHz CLASS flux densities gives a spectral index of =-0.25 0.11 . J0011+1446 is therefore a flat-spectrum source, although, because non-VLBI flux densities are only available at two frequencies, it is possible that it could also have a peaked or concave spectrum.

gmw_00004513.name: J0131 0321
gmw_00004513.overview: J0131 0321 is a radio-loud blazar [arXiv:1503.06593, arXiv:1803.01510] located at a high redshift of z=5.18 [arXiv:1503.06593]. The galaxy exhibits a flat radio spectrum [arXiv:1701.06622], strong thermal disk emission [arXiv:1803.01510], and likely flux variability [arXiv:1503.06593, arXiv:1701.06622].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J013127.34 032100.1 as a z=5.18 radio-bright quasar whose compact EVN detection, high brightness temperature, variability, and Doppler boosting indicate a blazar nature.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension _ t =01^ h 31^ m 27 3473 and declination _ t =-03^ 21 00 0791

quotes.quote: SDSS J013127.34 032100.1 (J0131 0321, hereafter). The source was first selected as a candidate high-redshift quasar sdss_selection using the optical--infrared selection criteria based on the Sloan Digital Sky Survey SDSS, sdss and Wide-Field Infrared Survey Explorer WISE , wise photometry. Its optical spectrum was first measured with the Yunnan Fainter Object Spectrograph and Camera FOSC in 2013 November. Later higher resolution optical and near-infrared spectra were obtained by the Magellan Echelette and Folded Port Infrared Echelette (FIRE) spectrographs in 2014 January. According to these measurements, the source has a redshift of z=5.18 0.01 disc .

Our EVN observation revealed a single, compact radio component (Fig. fig:EVN ). Using the phase-referenced data, we derived the position of the brightness peak with the maxfit verb in aips : right ascension _ t =01^ h 31^ m 27 3473 and declination _ t =-03^ 21 00 0791 . We estimate that each coordinate is accurate within 1 mas.

sources.summary: The paper includes SDSS J0131 0321 among radio-loud z>5 blazars, modeling its sparse SED, strong thermal disk emission, weak hard-X-ray constraints, and LAT Doppler-factor bounds.

quotes.quote: Comparison with other z>5 Blazars Since their SEDs are quite similar, we next make a comparative analysis for the other three claimed z>5 blazars B2 1023+25, SDSS J0131 0321, and SDSS J114657.79+403708.6 hereafter B2 1023, J0131, and J1146; stgp+13,gstf+14,gtsg15 . These sources are all radio loud (to varying degrees) and thus represent a population of high-mass, spin-dominated BHs in the early universe. We can update earlier characterization of the SEDs around the critical EC peak, by re-measuring the X-ray archival data and by deriving improved Fermi LAT upper limits by using 8 years of Pass-8 data.

The SEDs of J0131 and J1146 are even less well measured, but do show some differences from the other two. J0131 has very strong thermal disk emission and J1146 has relatively small SSC flux compared to its synchrotron emission. For the these blazars low frequency IR points are useful for estimating the synchrotron component flux, but the lack of hard X-ray measurements leaves the SCC peak frequency almost unconstrained. Thus _ e ( _ ssc,pk ^ obs / _ sy,pk ^ obs )^ 1/2 is similarly unconstrained. The improved LAT upper limits from our analysis do place a bound 16 in both cases, but as small as 4 seems acceptable (Figure fig:fig3 bottom).

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0131 0321 as flat-spectrum and likely variable, connecting its flat spectrum and Doppler-boosted VLBI emission.

quotes.quote: A power-law fit for the spectrum of J0131 0321 (Fig. fig:J0131 0321 ) gives =0.12 0.10 . J0131 0321 is therefore a flat-spectrum source, although, because non-VLBI flux densities are only available at two frequencies, it is possible that it could also have a peaked or concave spectrum. GCF2015(V) observed this source with the EVN at 1.7 GHz and found it to be unresolved, with a flux density of 64.4 0.3 mJy. Comparing this to the 1.4 GHz FIRST and NVSS flux densities of 33.7 1.7 and 31.4 1.0 mJy, respectively, GCF2015(V) concluded that J0131 0321 is likely variable. However, since the epochs when FIRST and NVSS observed J0131 0321 differ by about 15.25 years 2011ApJ...737...45O,2015ApJ...801...26H , if J0131 0321 is variable it means that the FIRST and NVSS observations were serendipitously done on two epochs when J0131 0321 happened to have the same flux density.

gmw_00004514.name: J0210 0018
gmw_00004514.overview: J0210 0018 is a high-redshift, radio-loud luminous quasar [arXiv:1701.06622, arXiv:astro-ph/0008122]. Radio observations reveal that the source consists of two components separated by 7.0 arcseconds, which are interpreted as an active galactic nucleus core and a lobe or hotspot [arXiv:1701.06622].

sources.summary: The paper finds J0210 0018 has two radio components, rejects lensing as likely, and interprets the southern component as the AGN core and northern component as a lobe or hotspot.

quotes.quote: J0210 0018 Fig. fig:J0210 0018 gmrt610 image and fig:J0210 0018 stripe82 image show the 608 MHz GMRT610 and 1.4 GHz VLA STRIPE82 images of J0210 0018. In both of these images the source has two components. Table tbl:J0210 0018 componet flux gives the flux densities of the individual components. Using the GMRT610 and STRIPE82 flux densities we calculate spectral indices of 0.79 0.21 and 0.36 0.13 for the northern and southern components, respectively. Using the STRIPE82 positions of the two components, the angular separation between the components is 7.0 arcsec, which translates to a linear separation of 45.6 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSSp J021043.17--001818.4 as a 9.75 mJy FIRST source and the highest-redshift radio-loud luminous quasar then known, notes its 10.4' separation from SDSSp J021102.72--000910.3, and reports no ROSAT detection.

quotes.quote: Three of them have FIRST counterparts at 20 cm at the 1 mJy level (with positional matches better than 1'' ): SDSSp J013108.19+005248.2 ( z=4.19 ), SDSSp J021043.17--001818.4 ( z=4.77 ) and SDSSp J030025.23+003224.2 ( z=4.19 ), correspond to unresolved FIRST sources of 4.00, 9.75 and 7.56 mJy at 20 cm, respectively. In particular, SDSSp J021043.17--001818.4 ( z=4.77 ) is the highest redshift radio-loud luminous quasar yet known. None of the objects was detected in the ROSAT full-sky pixel images (Voges et al. 1999) at a typical 3 upper limit of 3 10^ 13 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in the 0.1 -- 2.4 keV band.

SDSSp J021043.17--001818.4 ( z=4.77 ). This is the highest redshift radio loud quasar known to date, and is located only 10.4' away from SDSSp J021102.72--000910.3 ( z=4.90 ). The co-moving separation of the two quasars is 29.7 h^ 1 Mpc for =1 .

gmw_00004515.name: J1430+4204
gmw_00004515.overview: J1430+4204 is a high-redshift, flat-spectrum radio quasar and blazar located at a redshift of z = 4.72 [arXiv:1912.12597, arXiv:1701.06622]. The galaxy exhibits a compact core-jet structure with a kiloparsec-scale radio and X-ray jet [arXiv:1912.12597, arXiv:1406.4797], and it displays broad-band variability [arXiv:1912.12597, arXiv:1805.07765].

sources.summary: The paper presents 22 years of VLBI observations of high-redshift blazar J1430+4204, measuring a compact core-jet, fast apparent motion, and highly relativistic beaming.

quotes.quote: Here we report on very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) observations of a high-redshift blazar J1430+4204 ( z=4.72 ) in the 8 GHz frequency band at five different epochs spanning 22 years. The source shows a compact core--jet structure with two jet components being identified within 3 milli-arcsecond (mas) scale. The long time span and multiple-epoch data allow for the kinematic studies of the jet components. That results in a jet proper motion of (J1) = 0.017 0.002 mas yr ^ 1 and ( J2 ) =0.156 0.015 mas yr ^ 1 , respectively. For the fastest-moving outer jet component J2, the corresponding apparent transverse speed is 19.5 1.9 .

J1430+4204 was discovered as a radio-loud quasar at z=4.72 . Further X-ray and radio observations confirmed its blazar nature with broad-band variability and flat radio spectrum . A distant ( 3.6^ ) jet component was detected in the northwestern direction of the nucleus from Chandra X-ray and Very Large Array (VLA) radio observations, making J1430+4204 the most distant quasar being detected with kiloparsec (kpc) scale radio/X-ray jet known so far .

sources.summary: The paper reports BZQ J1430+4204 as the most distant new gamma-ray blazar candidate in the study, with multiple monthly TS detections and a soft Fermi/LAT spectrum.

quotes.quote: Multiple TS > 9 monthly detections are unlikely to happen by chance, and we have detected several individual new sources in this way, including the most distant ray blazar, BZQ J1430+4204 (z = 4.72). Finally, two new ray blazars at redshifts of z = 3.63 and z = 3.11 are unambiguously detected via very significant (TS > 25) flares in individual monthly time bins.

In the following, we choose the blazar BZQ J1430+4204 (z = 4.72) listed in Table High-Z sources TS 9 as an example of the statistical method used. This blazar has been identified in five monthly light curve intervals up to TS 20 . Following Eqn. P9 , this results in a Poisson probability of P_ N_s = 3.72 .

Figure SED displays the high-energy hump of the blazar 5BZQ J1430+4204, using Swift /XRT and Fermi /LAT data. The Fermi /LAT spectrum shows a soft power-law spectral index of 2.7 , which is also common for blazars in the local universe during flaring states. The entire ray emission is centered in the first spectral bin with TS 12 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies GB 1428+4217 as a radio-loud z>4 quasar with published X-ray data, relativistic beaming, and ROSAT variability consistent with a flat-spectrum radio-loud blazar.

quotes.quote: Three objects which we detected had already been reported as X-ray emitters in the past (Q 0000 2619, GB 1428+4217, and RX J1759.4+6638). The X-ray properties we have measured for them are in agreement with the previous reported properties (see Table 1). At present only a few radio-loud z>4 quasars are known. These include GB 1428+4217, GB 1508+5714, and RX J1028.6 0844 which have had their X-ray data published prior to this work (Table 1), PKS 1251 407, for which we present an X-ray upper limit, and GB 1713+2148, whose ROSAT observation is not yet public. Out of these objects, GB 1428+4217 and GB 1508+5714 show evidence for relativistic beaming (e.g., Moran Helfand 1997; Fabian et al. 1999).

The quasar GB 1428+4217 has six ROSAT observations and was found to vary by a factor of two over a timescale of two weeks (or less), which corresponds to less than 2.5 days in the source's rest-frame, a result not unexpected in that this quasar is a flat-spectrum radio-loud blazar (Fabian et al. 1998, 1999).

sources.summary: The paper notes GB 1428 + 4217 has extended radio and X-ray-associated emission, a mas-scale radio structure, an X-ray-peaking SED, and strong high-energy limits.

quotes.quote: The extended radio feature of GB 1428 + 4217 in its VLA imaging at observed-frame 1.4 GHz and 4.9 GHz (Cheung et al. 2012) lies 3.6^ away from the core. It also coincides with the extended \ emission found in its \ observation. GB 1428 + 4217 furthermore shows mas-scale extended radio emission in its VLBI images at observed-frame 2.3 GHz and 8.6 GHz.

The SEDs of RX J1028 0844, GB 1428 + 4217, and GB 1508 + 5714 all reach maximal L values (over the 10^9 - 10^ 19 Hz range) in the \ band. This type of SED has been modeled as the emission generated by Compton scattering of synchrotron emission from the jet itself and/or of a powerful external photon field such as the thermal emission of the accretion process (SSC/EC; see Fabian et al. 1998, 1999; Ghisellini et al. 1998).

sources.summary: The note identifies this as the highest-redshift quasar then known with kiloparsec-scale X-ray emission and describes a jet-related double structure.

quotes.quote: J1430+4204: This is currently the highest redshift ( z = 4.715 ) quasar to exhibit kiloparsec-scale X-ray emission . The weaker component of this double is coincident with the extended X-ray emission and is most likely a jet knot. The VIPS image at 5 GHz shows a very compact core with a slight extension to the south-west . The jet is much more prominent in the USNO observations at 2.3 GHz made by Fey et al. (2004) and re-imaged by Cheung ( op. cit. )

sources.summary: The paper treats BZQ J1430+4205 as a z=4.72 radio-loud, extremely X-ray luminous blazar with only marginal gamma-ray evidence.

quotes.quote: Here we present the results of a multi-wavelength study of the four high redshift FSRQs, namely, S5 0014+81 (hereafter J0014+81; z=3.37 ), CGRaBS J0225+1846 (hereafter J0225+1846; z=2.69 ), BZQ J1430+4205 (hereafter J1430+4205; z=4.72 ), and 3FGL J1656.2 3303 (hereafter J1656 3303; z=2.40 ).

At a redshift of 4.72, J1430+4205 is a radio-loud F_ 5 GHz =230 mJy; 2007ApJ...658..203H and an extremely X-ray luminous blazar e.g., 1997MNRAS.291L...5F,1998MNRAS.295L..25F,1998MNRAS.294L...7H . 1999MNRAS.308L...6F reported the detection of a significant radio and X-ray flux variability from this source and postulated the presence of billion solar mass black hole at the center. Later, XMM-Newton observations confirmed the presence of soft X-ray flattening in the X-ray spectrum of this object 2004MNRAS.350L..67W , a feature typically observed in many high redshift radio-loud quasars e.g., 2005MNRAS.364..195P . J1430+4205 was monitored by on 2014 July 14, thereby making it the second most distant source observed by after B2 1023+25 z=5.28 ; 2013ApJ...777..147S .

Apart from these, LAT data analysis of J0014+81 and J1430+4205 resulted in TS = 1.47 and 5.62, respectively. This suggests the non-detection of J0014+81 and only a marginal 2 detection of J1430+4205 in the ray band.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1428 + 422 as a z=4.72 high-redshift quasar with X-ray and radio jets, the highest luminosity in the sample, and a marginal/significant jet-region detection.

quotes.quote: Four flat-spectrum radio jets from the z>3.4 radio sample were observed in a \ AO8 program (PI: Cheung) from 2007 Jan - June (ObsIDs: 7871-7874): 1239 + 376 ( z=3.82 ), 1754 + 676 ( z=3.60 ), 1418 064 ( z=3.69 ), 1428 + 422 ( z=4.72 ), in addition to one quasar at z=2.10 (0833 + 585 ; ObsID 7870) which had a known long radio jet mur93 . The jets have minimum projected lengths of 2.5 \ and up to 15 \ for the z=2.1 case (Table 1), so their X-ray counterparts are easily separable from the bright nucleus in the \ images. The exposures of 3.8 - 11 ks were tailored to the radio jet brightness and redshifts.
The most luminous quasar in our sample (1428 + 422) has a 2-10 keV luminosity of 4.0 0.2 10^ 47 erg s ^ 1 .

For example, ROI 1 in source 1428 + 422 (ObsID 7874) is a marginal detection with an upper bound on the p value of 0.010, but decreasing the region size from 91 to 77 pixels, an arguably better fit for this object in radio, results in an improvement in the p value upper bound to 0.009, which crosses the threshold into a significant detection.

sources.summary: The paper concludes J1430+4204 is extremely variable and, combining its average flat spectral index with Doppler boosting, classifies it as a flat-spectrum radio quasar.

quotes.quote: J1430+4204 WFP2006 observed J1430+4204 at 15.2 GHz over a period of 7.5 years, during which time they found the flux density to vary between 70 and 430 mJy. Based on these findings and the spectrum of J1430+4204 (Fig. fig:J1430+4204 ), we conclude that J1430+4204 is extremely variable. Fig. fig:J1430+4204 gives the average 15.2 GHz WFP2006 flux density. Fitting a power law to the spectrum, we find a spectral index of 0.10 0.03 . While this spectral index is likely not a good indication of the spectral index of the source at any given time, it can be considered as an average spectral index. Combining this with the finding that J1430+4204 is Doppler-boosted 2016MNRAS.tmp.1343C , we conclude that J1430+4204 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar.

sources.summary: The paper identifies B3 1428+422 as a high-redshift blazar with a likely transient GeV counterpart, making it a candidate for the most distant detected GeV source.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of a transient ray source in the direction of B3 1428+422 ( z=4.72 ) by analyzing the 110-month Pass 8 data. The new transient ray source is far away from the Galactic plane and has a rather soft spectrum, in agreement with being a high redshift blazar. We suggest that the newly discovered transient is the ray counterpart of B3 1428+422, which could be the most distant GeV source detected so far.

B3 1428+422 ( z =4.72, 1998MNRAS.294L...7H ), also known as GB 1428+4217, was identified in an optical spectroscopy search for high redshift flat-spectrum radio sources. Its high X-ray luminosity ( 10^ 47 erg s^ 1 , 1997MNRAS.291L...5F ), the hard X-ray spectrum ( _ x 1.5, 2004MNRAS.350L..67W ), the radio morphology including a compact dominant core with high brightness temperature ( T_ b 5 10^ 11 K, 2010A&A...521A...6V ) and more importantly the significant radio and X-ray variability 1999MNRAS.308L...6F,2006MNRAS.368..844W strongly suggest that B3 1428+422 is a highly active high redshift blazar.

gmw_00004516.name: J0813+3508
gmw_00004516.overview: J0813+3508 is a high-redshift, radio-loud quasar [arXiv:1810.06572, arXiv:2203.09527] powered by a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2203.09527]. It features a one-sided radio jet [arXiv:1810.06572] pointing toward a nearby lobe-like component [arXiv:1701.06622], but it lacks a corresponding extended X-ray structure [arXiv:1810.06572].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J 0813+3508 as a high-redshift radio-loud quasar with resolved radio emission, a one-sided VLBI jet, and no convincing extended X-ray jet.

quotes.quote: The remaining two (SDSS J0813+3508 and SDSS J2220+0025) are resolved into multiple components Becker1995, Hodge2011 and have a linear extent of 10'' ( 70 kpc). The radio flux of the extended component of SDSS J0813+3508 is about half that of the core, while the extended radio component is brighter than the core for SDSS J2220+0025.

At such small scales, these quasars are often mildly resolved and show a compact core with a one-sided jet (SDSS J 0813+3508 and PMN J 2134-0419 ) or an unresolved core (SDSS J 1242+5422 and SDSS J 2220+0025 ).

We then compared the radio and X-ray images of SDSS J 0813+3508 , and did not find extended X-ray structure that corresponds to the radio jet, as shown in Fig.  fig:radioXimg (centre and right). Note that the extended radio component that is 7'' away from SDSS J 0813+3508 has a peak radio flux density (11.9 mJy) that is about half that of the core (20.0 mJy). We estimated the (observed-frame 0.5--2  keV) surface brightness coincident with the extended radio component to be <1.43 10^ 16 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 arcsec ^ 2 , which means that the extended jet (if it exists) must be more than 10 times dimmer than the core in X-rays.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0813+3508 as a steep-spectrum source with a nearby lobe-like component, supported by EVN jet evidence and FIRST flux measurements.

quotes.quote: J0813+3508 In FIRST (1.4 GHz) there is a second source due northwest of the source matched to J0813+3508 that is 6.9 arcsec distant from the J0813+3508 VLBI position, which translates to a linear size of 43.7 kpc. FPG2010(V) observed both sources with the EVN at 1.7 and 5 GHz. While the second source was not detected, the authors did find a jet pointing from J0813+3508 towards the second source in the 1.7 GHz image. From this, FPG2010(V) concluded that the second source is a lobe of J0813+3508 that is resolved out by the VLBI observations. The only non-VLBI observation that has high enough resolution to resolve the two components is FIRST, in which the main and second components have flux densities of 37.5 1.9 and 11.5 0.6 mJy, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper models SDSS J081333.32+350810.8 as a high-redshift radio-loud quasar with a 4e9 solar-mass black hole, moderate Eddington ratio, and likely edge-of-blazar viewing angle.

quotes.quote: SDSS J081333.32+350810.8 --- The BH mass is 4 10^9 M_ with L_ d =12 L_ Edd . It can be fitted with _ v =5.2^ , that is 1/ , if =13 , but also with _ v =8^ (with the same ). Therefore it is likely a source viewed at the edge of our definition of blazar.

gmw_00004517.name: J0324 2918
gmw_00004517.overview: J0324 2918 is a high-redshift radio galaxy and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1701.06622]. It is classified as a peaked-spectrum source, with a spectral turnover located at either 1.4 GHz or 7 GHz [arXiv:1701.06622].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0324 2918 as peaked-spectrum, with possible variability and Doppler-boosted VLBI emission explaining discrepant high-frequency fluxes.

quotes.quote: J0324 2918 There is a discrepancy between the 4.8 and 8.6 GHz AT20G flux densities, and the 8.4 GHz CRATES and 4.9 GHz PMN flux densities in the spectrum of J0324 2918 (Fig. fig:J0324 2918 ). Regardless of which set of points are considered, it is clear from the 148 MHz TGSS flux density that J0324 2918 is a peaked-spectrum source. The spectral turnover would be at 1.4 GHz or 7 GHz (depending on which observations are considered). There are two possible explanations for the discrepancy in flux densities between these observations. First, the AT20G values are peak brightnesses, rather than integrated flux densities. Second, the AT20G observations have a resolution between 30 and 2 arcsec 2010MNRAS.402.2403M , the 4.9 GHz PMN observations have a resolution of 4.2 arcmin and we could not determine the resolution of the 8.4 GHz CRATES observations.

gmw_00004518.name: J1659+2101
gmw_00004518.overview: J1659+2101 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:1701.06622] classified as a blazar [arXiv:2203.09527]. It hosts a supermassive black hole with a mass of 2.5 billion solar masses [arXiv:2203.09527] and exhibits a peaked radio spectrum [arXiv:1701.06622].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1659+2101 as peaked-spectrum after finding an unexplained low-frequency flux discrepancy that cannot be attributed to systematic offsets or resolution effects.

quotes.quote: J1659+2101 The 148 MHz TGSS and 147 MHz GMRT150 flux densities in the spectrum of J1659+2101 (Fig. fig:J1659+2101 ) are 27.6 5.7 and 48.2 5.4 mJy, respectively. This translates to a difference of 1.9 or 75 per cent in flux density. Visual inspection of the images did not reveal an explanation for the offset. To try find an explanation, we matched the sources in the 147 MHz GMRT150 image to those in TGSS using a 10 arcsec search radius. We found 22 matches within a square of 1 1 deg centred on J1659+2101. For each of these sources, we calculated the ratio between the 147 MHz GMRT150 and the 148 MHz TGSS flux densities: The median of all of the ratios was 0.95, and the average was 1.02.

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS J165913.23+210115.8 as a blazar with a 2.5e9 solar-mass black hole, 10 percent Eddington accretion, and hard luminous X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: SDSS J165913.23+210115.8 --- The BH mass is M=2.5 10^9 M_ with L_ d =10 L_ Edd . The X--ray spectrum and luminosity clearly suggest the blazar nature of this source, confirmed by our model with _ v =3^ and =13 .

gmw_00004519.name: J1454+1109
gmw_00004519.overview: J1454+1109 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1609.00575] that is classified as a likely blazar [arXiv:1701.06622]. The source exhibits flux variability [arXiv:1609.00575, arXiv:1701.06622] and features Doppler-boosted radio emission [arXiv:1609.00575, arXiv:1701.06622].

sources.summary: The paper concludes J1454+1109 is variable from EVN, FIRST, and NVSS fluxes and classifies it as an archetypal flat-spectrum radio quasar with Doppler boosting.

quotes.quote: From Table tbl:Flux density ratios , the EVN flux density of J1454+1109 is higher than its FIRST flux density, which is higher than its NVSS flux density. We therefore conclude that J1454+1109 is variable.
J1454+1109 is the archetypal example of a FSRQ, having a flat spectrum over a wide spectral range, showing variability and being Doppler-boosted.

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1454+1109 as likely a blazar, citing variability, Doppler-boosted VLBI emission, and an unconstrained but likely flat time-averaged spectrum.

quotes.quote: J1454+1109 Based on the VLBI flux densities being higher than the non-VLBI flux densities in the spectrum of J1454+1109 (Fig. fig:J1454+1109 ), and the 4.9 GHz GB6 upper limit and the 1.4 GHz FIRST flux density being higher than the 1.4 GHz NVSS flux density, we conclude that J1454+1109 is variable. In addition, due to a lack of spectral coverage, we cannot constrain the spectrum. However, based on its variability, and the fact that the VLBI emission is Doppler-boosted 2016MNRAS.tmp.1343C , J1454+1109 is likely a blazar with a flat time-averaged spectrum.

gmw_00004520.name: J1611+0844
gmw_00004520.overview: J1611+0844 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:1701.06622]. Because its VLBI flux densities exceed its non-VLBI flux densities, the galaxy is likely variable, though its time-averaged radio spectrum remains spectrally ambiguous [arXiv:1701.06622].

sources.summary: The paper leaves J1611+0844 spectrally ambiguous but argues variability is likely because VLBI flux densities exceed non-VLBI flux densities.

quotes.quote: J1611+0844 Assuming that the spectrum of J1611+0844 (Fig. fig:J1611+0844 ) can be fitted with a single power law, and using the 1.4 GHz FIRST flux density and the 148 MHz TGSS and 4.9 GHz GB6 upper limits, 0.06< <0.57 . The time-averaged spectrum can therefore be either inverted, flat or peaked. Since the VLBI flux densities are higher than the non-VLBI flux densities, it is likely that J1611+0844 is variable. However, since the epochs when FIRST and NVSS (1.4 ,GHz) observed J1611+0844 differ by about 3.6 years 2011ApJ...737...45O,2015ApJ...801...26H , if J1611+0844 is variable it means that the FIRST and NVSS observations were serendipitously done on two epochs when J1611+0844 happened to have the same flux density.

gmw_00004521.name: DLA2233+131
gmw_00004521.overview: DLA2233+131 is a high-redshift damped Lyman-alpha absorber galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.15 [arXiv:astro-ph/0401051, arXiv:2001.05604]. It is surrounded by an extended Lyman-alpha emission nebula [arXiv:astro-ph/0401051, arXiv:astro-ph/0401053] that is likely produced by a star-formation-powered outflow [arXiv:astro-ph/0401051].

sources.summary: The paper detects extended Lyman-alpha emission around the Q2233+131 DLA galaxy, interpreting it as a star-formation-powered outflow rather than a rotating disk.

quotes.quote: This paper presents observations of an extended Lyman- emission nebula surrounding the galaxy responsible for the Damped Lyman- Absorption (DLA) line in the spectrum of the quasar Q2233+131 . With the Potsdam Multi Aperture Spectrophotometer (PMAS) we measure the properties of the extended \ emission in an area of 3 5 \ having a total line flux of (2.8 0.3) 10^ 16 , which at redshift z=3.15 corresponds to a luminosity of ( 2.4_ 0.2 ^ +0.3 ) 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 and a size of 23 38 kpc. The location of the emission is spatially coincident with the previously detected DLA galaxy, but extends significantly beyond its limb.

We argue that the \ emission is likely to be caused by an outflow from the DLA galaxy, presumably powered by star formation. In the case of negligible dust extinction, the \ luminosity indicates a star-formation rate of 19 10 M _ yr ^ 1 consistent with that derived from the UV continuum flux from the parent galaxy. The wind velocity indicated by the integral field spectra is of the order of several hundred km s ^ 1 . We find no indication of emission originating in a rotating disk.

sources.summary: The Q2233+131 DLA galaxy was previously confirmed at a 17 kpc impact parameter; PMAS data reveal an extended emission nebula around it with larger flux than the discovery slit spectrum.

quotes.quote: Q2233+131 This QSO at z=3.29 has a broad absorption line from a z=3.15 neutral cloud with a column density of N_ H I =10^ 20 cm ^ 2 , i.e. lower than the classical limit of DLAs. Djorgovski et al. (1996) identified the galaxy responsible for the absorption line at an impact parameter of 2 5 using long slit spectroscopy with the Keck telescope. This distance corresponds to 17 kpc in a flat dominated cosmology. Given the fact that the DLA galaxy was already confirmed, it provided an ideal target for PMAS in order to investigate the limitations of the instrument for observing faint targets.

The data analysis has revealed that a nebula of \ emission at z=3.1538 surrounds the confirmed DLA galaxy, see Fig. fig:map (Christensen et al. 2004). The spectra of the QSO and the nebula are shown in Fig. fig:spec , indicating the correlation between the position of the DLA line in the spectrum and the emission line from the extended nebula. We find that the nebula has an extension of 3 5 , corresponding to 20 40 kpc at the redshift of the absorber, and the total flux from the source is 4 times larger than found in the discovery long slit spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper studies DLA2233+131 as an optically identified high-redshift DLA galaxy counterpart, using ALMA Band 8 to search for [C II] while discussing its Ly-alpha emission and absorption properties.

quotes.quote: Since one possible origin of DLAs at high redshift is an outflowing gas from star-forming galaxies, DLA2233+131 provides a good laboratory to investigate the nature of high- z DLAs. Motivated by this, we have carried out ALMA band 8 observations to study the [ C II ] line in this system. However, we do not detect any significant emission line in the observed pass bands.

Our target is DLA2233+131 found toward the quasar Q2233+131 at z=3.295 (see Table tab:info ). This is the first intervening DLA whose optical counterpart was identified in both Ly emission and stellar continuum by 1996Natur.382..234D . The H i column density of DLA2233+131 has been measured as follows; log N (H i ) (cm ^ 2 ) = 20.00 1993ApJS...84....1L , 20.2 2002PASA...19..455C , and 19.95 [ SDSS DR5; see 2014ApJ...780..116K ] .

gmw_00004522.name: SMG1
gmw_00004522.overview: SMG1 is a newly identified submillimeter galaxy located 4.7 arcseconds away from the quasar Q2233+131 [arXiv:2001.05604]. It is optically undetected, suggesting it is either a high-redshift galaxy or a very dusty galaxy at an intermediate redshift [arXiv:2001.05604].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMG1 as a newly found optically undetected submillimeter galaxy near Q2233+131, brighter at 646 microns than the quasar and possibly high-redshift or dusty.

quotes.quote: Instead, we have serendipitously found three submm continuum sources in the observed sky area. One appears to be the quasar Q2233+131 itself while the other two sources are newly identified submm galaxies (SMGs), called SMG1 and SMG2 in this paper. They are located at a separation of 4 ^ .7 and 8 ^ .1 from Q2233+131, respectively. Their 646 m fluxes are 6.35 mJy and 6.43 mJy, respectively, being higher than that of Q2233+131, 3.62 mJy. Since these two SMGs are not detected in the optical images obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope and the Subaru Telescope, they have a very red spectral energy distribution. It is, therefore, suggested that they are high-redshift galaxies or very dusty galaxies at intermediate redshift although we cannot rule out the possibility that they are optically very faint SMG analogs at low redshift.

gmw_00004523.name: SMG2
gmw_00004523.overview: SMG2 is a newly identified submillimeter galaxy located 8.1 arcseconds from the quasar Q2233+131 [arXiv:2001.05604]. It is optically undetected and exhibits a very red spectral energy distribution, suggesting it is either a high-redshift galaxy or a highly dusty galaxy at an intermediate redshift [arXiv:2001.05604].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMG2 as a newly found optically undetected submillimeter galaxy near Q2233+131, with a 646 micron flux comparable to SMG1 and a very red SED.

quotes.quote: Instead, we have serendipitously found three submm continuum sources in the observed sky area. One appears to be the quasar Q2233+131 itself while the other two sources are newly identified submm galaxies (SMGs), called SMG1 and SMG2 in this paper. They are located at a separation of 4 ^ .7 and 8 ^ .1 from Q2233+131, respectively. Their 646 m fluxes are 6.35 mJy and 6.43 mJy, respectively, being higher than that of Q2233+131, 3.62 mJy. Since these two SMGs are not detected in the optical images obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope and the Subaru Telescope, they have a very red spectral energy distribution. It is, therefore, suggested that they are high-redshift galaxies or very dusty galaxies at intermediate redshift although we cannot rule out the possibility that they are optically very faint SMG analogs at low redshift.

gmw_00004524.name: 5BZQ J1429+5406
gmw_00004524.overview: 5BZQ J1429+5406 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1203.6071] and a newly detected gamma-ray blazar [arXiv:2009.07945]. The object is a core-dominated double source featuring a 30 milliarcsecond radio jet [arXiv:1406.4797].

sources.summary: The paper singles out 5BZQ J1429+5406 as the only newly detected gamma-ray blazar with both X-ray and gamma-ray observations available for high-energy SED comparison.

quotes.quote: Swift /XRT data analysis In our sample of significantly detected new ray blazars listed in Table High-Z sources TS 9 , 5BZQ J1429+5406 is the only source for which Swift /XRT data are available. These data were reduced with standard methods, using the software package HEASOFT v. 6.26.1. The data were reprocessed with the xrtpipeline tool using standard quality cuts. Since no significant variability is evident within the available observations, the spectra were summed using the addspec tool. The X-ray spectrum was grouped to a S/N of 1 using the grppha tool and then fitted using Cash statistics.

sources.summary: The paper says SDSSJ142921.87 + 540611.2 is an FSRQ and lensed-quasar candidate, while noting a previous rejection of the lensing interpretation.

quotes.quote: SDSSJ142921.87 + 540611.2 . This object is classified as an FSRQ by hea07 , and mas09 , and listed as a lensed quasar candidate by kin99 . bro03 rejected the lensing hypothesis based upon surface brightness and spectral indices criteria.

sources.summary: The note describes a very small core-dominated double with a steeper-than-usual core spectral index and a 30 mas VIPS jet.

quotes.quote: J1429+5406: This is a very small double source. Although it is core-dominated, the core spectral index is steeper than usual ( = -0.4 ). The VIPS image shows a 30 mas jet in position angle 140^ .

gmw_00004525.name: 5BZQ J2219 2719
gmw_00004525.overview: 5BZQ J2219 2719 is a highly radio-loud flat-spectrum radio quasar and blazar located at a redshift of approximately 3.63 [arXiv:2106.00100, arXiv:2009.07945]. The source exhibits monthly gamma-ray variability [arXiv:2106.00100, arXiv:2009.07945] and features a radio jet coincident with extended X-ray emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0601037].

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J2219 2719 as the highest-redshift 1FLT FSRQ, detected in one temporal bin with low duty cycle, high Compton dominance, and monthly variability.

quotes.quote: The highest redshift FSRQ: PMN J2219 2719 1FLT J2219 2732 is positionally consistent with PMN J2219 2719 and was detected in TBIN 96 (2016 August 04 to 2016 September 04) of our analysis. PMN J2219 2719 is a well-known FSRQ belonging to the Fifth Roma-BZCAT catalog massaro2014optical at a redshift of z = 3.634 hook2002discovery . This source has a very low duty cycle (see section DC ) of about 2.5 and it lies at the highest redshift of the entire 1FLT catalog. Examination of the SED (see Figure figure:PMNJ2219-2719 ) reveals a large CD_ of 1.8 during a period of flaring activity.

This behavior is typical of FSRQs and is usually interpreted as an external Compton process (see Figure 11 of abdo2010pks ). PMN J2219 2719 is also identified as the most distant TS > 25 blazar with monthly variability detected in kreter2020search .

sources.summary: The paper uses 5BZQ J2219 2719 as an example of a stable-position monthly gamma-ray detection and identifies it as a z=3.63 high-redshift blazar.

quotes.quote: In this way, a contamination of the observed ray flux from other sources was prevented. TS maps were calculated for each monthly interval. Figure TS map 1 shows such a map for the blazar 5BZQ J2219 2719 at z = 3.63. Monthly intervals for which no emission could be associated to the high- z blazar best-fit position were not considered for further analysis. Fig. TS map 2 shows such an excluded interval for the blazar 5BZQ J1337 1319 at z = 3.48. Such excluded intervals are caused by a misidentification from a bright nearby ray emitter.

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J2219 2719 as a highly radio-loud high-redshift quasar with slight extended X-ray counts coincident with a prominent radio jet.

quotes.quote: Two of our targets are not as representative: PMN J1230 1139 and PMN J2219 2719 are highly radio-loud with R 7400--9500 . However, these targets are important as they enlarge the radio-loud ``blazar'' population with X-ray observations (only 5 blazars have been detected in X-rays previously).

PSF analysis of the PMN J1451 1512 and PMN J2219 2719 snapshots displayed a slight excess of counts between 1 and 2 arcseconds from the core. The raw X-ray image of PMN J2219 2719 showed six counts extended to the South ( 3 of the total X-ray flux from the source) which are coincident with extended radio emission observed from the source (C. C. Cheung et al., in preparation).
Recent radio work on PMN J2219 2719 (C. C. Cheung et al., in preparation) shows that jets cannot be stifled totally as the source has a prominent radio jet coincident with extended X-ray emission.

gmw_00004526.name: RGG I
gmw_00004526.overview: RGG I is a dwarf galaxy that is classified as an ambiguous active galactic nucleus host [arXiv:1605.05731]. Broad hydrogen emission is detected in its spectra, but the measured line widths are inconsistent across different observation epochs [arXiv:1605.05731].

sources.summary: RGG I is classed as ambiguous because broad emission appears in all three spectra, but its DIS FWHM depends strongly on the narrow-line model and is inconsistent across epochs.

quotes.quote: RGG I (NSA 112250) has broad emission detected in the SDSS, DIS, and OSMOS spectra, but the FWHM measured for the DIS spectrum is highly dependent on the narrow-line model used, i.e., changing the parameters of the narrow-line model changes the measured FWHM by up to 90 . The FWHM is also not consistent between the three spectra ( 1237 69 , 316 27 , and 1717 32 for SDSS, DIS, and OSMOS, respectively). Additional observations of all three ambiguous broad H targets will be necessary to determine whether these objects do indeed host AGN.

gmw_00004527.name: RGG K
gmw_00004527.overview: RGG K is a dwarf galaxy that is classified as an active galactic nucleus host [arXiv:1605.05731]. It is considered an ambiguous broad-line source because asymmetric broad H-alpha emission is visible in some spectra but not required in others [arXiv:1605.05731].

sources.summary: RGG K is another ambiguous broad-line dwarf galaxy: its SDSS and OSMOS spectra show an asymmetric broad line, while DIS does not require the component.

quotes.quote: Finally, RGG K (NSA 91579) has a highly asymmetric broad line observed in the SDSS and OSMOS spectra. However, the DIS spectrum, taken in between the SDSS and OSMOS observations, does not require a broad component. We do observe asymmetric H emission by eye in the all three spectra. Similarly to with RGG B, we cannot rule out that there is indeed persistent broad H emission, and that the DIS spectrum does not require it because of issues relating to the fitting of the unusually shaped narrow lines.

gmw_00004528.name: RGG J
gmw_00004528.overview: RGG J is a galaxy that has been classified as containing an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1208.1303, arXiv:1605.05731]. However, its broad hydrogen emission is transient and likely supernova-related [arXiv:1605.05731], while its host galaxy exhibits substantial star formation [arXiv:1208.1303].

sources.summary: RGG J is treated as a cautionary transient source: despite previous broad-line AGN classifications, the authors classify its broad H emission as transient and likely supernova-related.

quotes.quote: In the following, we discuss the interesting case of RGG J. This particular source, classified in this work as having transient broad H emission, has fooled many of us. It was identified as a broad line AGN in , , and . Thus, it serves as an excellent cautionary tale that there may well be transient sources lurking in our broad-line AGN samples even at higher mass. We also have more epochs for this target than others.

It was observed twice by the SDSS, once by (spectrum taken in 2008), and once by us in 2013. The SDSS spectra were taken on 11 March 2003 and 05 April 2003 (see Figure sdss_j ). Thus, our observations span a full decade.

sources.summary: The paper says J1143+5500 falls in the star-forming region of the diagnostic diagram; broad lines confirm an AGN, but host star formation likely contaminates its Balmer emission.

quotes.quote: Two of our galaxies, J1143+5500 and J2156+1103, fall into the star-forming region as denoted by the Kauffman et al. dashed line, indicating contamination from H 2 regions within the host galaxies. While the presence of broad emission lines in our objects confirms the presence of an AGN in these two galaxies, they must have substantial contributions from star formation within the host galaxy.
However, only two sources, J1143+5500 and J2156+1103, lie far enough from the AGN locus to be consistent with this level of contamination (Fig. contour ).

gmw_00004529.name: NGC692
gmw_00004529.overview: NGC 692 is a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2311.14254]. The galaxy features a barred structure that influences its radial diffuse ionized gas behavior and enhances the surface brightness of its H II regions within the bar [arXiv:2311.14254].

sources.summary: BETIS includes NGC692 as an LLAGN showcase galaxy; its bar affects radial DIG behavior, and its regions show enhanced surface brightness within the bar.

quotes.quote: The subsample includes 3 known AGNs: NGC863 and NGC3393 are Seyfert 2 1977ARA&A..15...69W, 1988SoSAO..55....5L , and NGC692 which is a Low-Luminosity AGN (LLAGN; 2019ApJ...872..134Z ). The presence of a barred structure in NGC692, NGC6627, and ESO325-43 is evident through a decline in both distributions between 0.2-0.4 R _ 25 , which decreases up to 20 . This effect is also reflected on the radial distributions of the surface brightness (Fig. fig:SB_radial ), which show an increment of the for the regions distribution, reaching up to 10 ^ 39 erg s ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 between 0.2-0.4 R _ 25 for those galaxies.
However, there is an increase in within the bars of NGC692 and NGC6627 for the regions.

gmw_00004530.name: NGC6627
gmw_00004530.overview: NGC6627 is a barred Sb-type galaxy [arXiv:2311.14254]. Within its bar structure, the galaxy exhibits a decline in diffuse ionized gas distributions alongside an increase in H II region surface brightness [arXiv:2311.14254].

sources.summary: BETIS reports NGC6627 as a barred showcase galaxy whose radial DIG distributions decline across the bar while H-region surface brightness increases there.

quotes.quote: The presence of a barred structure in NGC692, NGC6627, and ESO325-43 is evident through a decline in both distributions between 0.2-0.4 R _ 25 , which decreases up to 20 . This effect is also reflected on the radial distributions of the surface brightness (Fig. fig:SB_radial ), which show an increment of the for the regions distribution, reaching up to 10 ^ 39 erg s ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 between 0.2-0.4 R _ 25 for those galaxies. We found that the galaxies with lower median in the DIG regions ( 9 A ) are NGC863 and NGC3393, both Sa type, followed by NGC692 (Sbc), NGC6627 (Sb) and ESO325-43 (Sc), with 18-20 A .
However, there is an increase in within the bars of NGC692 and NGC6627 for the regions.

gmw_00004531.name: ESO325-43
gmw_00004531.overview: ESO325-43 is a barred Sc-type galaxy [arXiv:2311.14254]. The galaxy exhibits a decline in diffuse ionized gas distributions between 0.2 and 0.4 R_25, which is a characteristic effect of its barred structure [arXiv:2311.14254].

sources.summary: BETIS treats ESO325-43 as a barred Sc showcase galaxy with moderate DIG equivalent width and a DIG-fraction pattern affected by its bar.

quotes.quote: The native spatial resolutions vary from 49 pc (IC3476, i.e. PHANGS-like) to 960 pc (ESO325-43, CALIFA-like), with a median resolution of 395 pc. The presence of a barred structure in NGC692, NGC6627, and ESO325-43 is evident through a decline in both distributions between 0.2-0.4 R _ 25 , which decreases up to 20 . This effect is also reflected on the radial distributions of the surface brightness (Fig. fig:SB_radial ), which show an increment of the for the regions distribution, reaching up to 10 ^ 39 erg s ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 between 0.2-0.4 R _ 25 for those galaxies.
We found that the galaxies with lower median in the DIG regions ( 9 A ) are NGC863 and NGC3393, both Sa type, followed by NGC692 (Sbc), NGC6627 (Sb) and ESO325-43 (Sc), with 18-20 A .

gmw_00004532.name: ESO584-7
gmw_00004532.overview: ESO584-7 is a spiral Sc galaxy [arXiv:2311.14254]. It exhibits a high star formation rate and luminosity, which likely drive its exceptionally high diffuse ionized gas equivalent width and high H II region surface brightness [arXiv:2311.14254].

sources.summary: BETIS finds ESO584-7 has one of the lowest DIG fractions but high H-region surface brightness and unusually high DIG equivalent width, likely linked to star formation.

quotes.quote: The Sc galaxy ESO584-7 and the dwarf IBm galaxy IC3476 present the lower DIG fraction, 0.37-0.66 and 0.4-0.63 respectively. Additionally, they have the highest surface brightness for the regions in the subsample, being >10 ^ 39 erg s ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 in the inner parts of the galaxy and decreasing to 5 10^ 38 at 0.5R _ 25 . ESO584-7 (Sc) and IC3476 (Im) have exceptionally high in the DIG regions, with 34 and 36 A respectively.
ESO584-7 is a galaxy 1998A&AS..130..285C , thus, the high luminosity and star formation rate may be the cause of this high median .

gmw_00004533.name: IC3476
gmw_00004533.overview: IC3476 is a dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:2311.14254] that is undergoing ram pressure stripping within the Virgo Cluster [arXiv:2311.14254, arXiv:2205.01695]. This interaction has compressed its gas to enhance star formation [arXiv:2311.14254] and created a trailing tail of ionized gas [arXiv:2306.04513].

sources.summary: BETIS describes IC3476 as a dwarf IBm showcase galaxy with low DIG fraction, high H-region surface brightness, and ram-pressure-related star formation enhancement.

quotes.quote: The native spatial resolutions vary from 49 pc (IC3476, i.e. PHANGS-like) to 960 pc (ESO325-43, CALIFA-like), with a median resolution of 395 pc. The Sc galaxy ESO584-7 and the dwarf IBm galaxy IC3476 present the lower DIG fraction, 0.37-0.66 and 0.4-0.63 respectively. Additionally, they have the highest surface brightness for the regions in the subsample, being >10 ^ 39 erg s ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 in the inner parts of the galaxy and decreasing to 5 10^ 38 at 0.5R _ 25 .

The case of IC3476 is also special. It is a galaxy suffering the effects of the ram pressure stripping due to the Virgo Cluster intergalactic environment 2021A&A...646A.139B . Furthermore, the compression of the gas along its stellar disc may be the cause of the increase of the star formation activity.

sources.summary: The paper favors UGC 7695 as a candidate source for BC4 and BC5 because its stripping wake and metallicity match, despite the large projected separation.

quotes.quote: Upon searching further afield we immediately identified UGC 7695 (VCC 1450, IC 3476) as a strong candidate. This galaxy is a well-studied example of ram pressure stripping in action Boselli+2021 , and has a prominent bow-shaped wake extending in the approximate direction of BC4 and BC5. Existing measurements of the metallicity of UGC 7695 Hughes+2012,Boselli+2021 also approximately match those of BC4 and BC5, making this a promising candidate for their point of origin. As the separation between the BCs and UGC 7695 is approximately 450 kpc in projection they would presumably require a very large (perhaps over 1000 ) ejection velocity, depending on when the stripping episode began.

sources.summary: The paper says VCC 1450 has ionized gas tails from recent ram-pressure onset and reports the tail in radio continuum for the first time.

quotes.quote: VCC 1450 (IC 3476) Boselli2021Astrophysics3476 reported the presence of ionized gas tails for VCC 1450, caused by a recent onset of ram-pressure. This is in agreement with the LOFAR images, where a strong gradient on the leading (eastern) side and a tail on the trailing side is visible. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time this tail is reported in the radio continuum.

gmw_00004534.overview: 0110+152 is a wide-angle tail radio galaxy located in the poor Abell 160 cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0501432]. It features a jet and counter-jet with a brightness ratio of 1.1 and an estimated inclination angle of 3.6 degrees [arXiv:astro-ph/0501432].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 0110+152 as Abell 160's WAT radio galaxy, measures its jet and counter-jet ratio, and finds projection effects do not substantially change the inferred jet length.

quotes.quote: Abell 160 hosts the WAT 0110+152. Abell 2462 is another poor Abell cluster.

Using the radio maps, we find jet/counter-jet ratios of 1.1 for 0110+152 and 2.3 for 2236-176. These give inclination angles of 3.6 and 33 deg for 0110+152 and 2236-176 respectively, which imply that the deprojected jet lengths would be 40 arcsec for 0110+152 and 33 arcsec for 2236-176. As expected, the projection effects are not great, since the jets and counter-jets in both sources are of approximately equal brightness, and so projection does not drastically change any of our conclusions here.

gmw_00004535.overview: The wide-angle tailed radio galaxy 2236-176 is hosted by the galaxy cluster Abell 2462 [arXiv:astro-ph/0501432]. It features a pair of well-collimated, asymmetrical jets that bend with S-symmetry [arXiv:astro-ph/0312245] before flaring into diffuse plumes, with the eastern plume exhibiting a sudden 90-degree bend [arXiv:astro-ph/0312245, arXiv:astro-ph/0501432].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2236 176 as a WAT with a 90-degree eastern-plume bend, S-symmetric collimated jets, and little compact structure at the plume bases.

quotes.quote: 2236 176 This source was previously observed by O'Donoghue \ (1990), and our low-resolution image (Fig. \ 2236l ) shows similar features; the noteworthy feature of the source is the 90^ bend in the E plume. On smaller scales, our high-resolution image (Fig. \ 2236h ) shows a well-collimated, asymmetrical pair of jets (NJ1, SJ1) which bend with S-symmetry at about the same distance from the nucleus (NJ2, SJ2). The fainter N jet appears to retain its collimation (NJ3) until it disappears into a diffuse region of high-surface-brightness emission (N1) with no obvious compact features at the base of the plume.

The S jet is similar, although there is a faint compact feature (S1) which may be associated with the jet termination. There are compact (comparable in size to the jet width), relatively bright features associated with the jet termination in 0647+693 (N jet), 1231+674 (N jet), 1333+412 (both jets) and 1433+553 (possibly both jets) but not clearly in 2236 176, 3C 465 or 1610 608, in which the jets simply make a smooth transition into the base of the plumes.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2236-176 as Abell 2462's WAT, describes its flaring jets and bent eastern lobe, and estimates inclination and deprojected jet length from radio maps.

quotes.quote: Abell 2462 hosts the WAT 2236-176. Both sets of data show narrow, well--collimated jets that flare into diffuse straight plumes. Additionally, the eastern lobe in 2236-176 bends suddenly through 90 degrees, 25 arcsec from the jet flaring point.

Using the radio maps, we find jet/counter-jet ratios of 1.1 for 0110+152 and 2.3 for 2236-176. These give inclination angles of 3.6 and 33 deg for 0110+152 and 2236-176 respectively, which imply that the deprojected jet lengths would be 40 arcsec for 0110+152 and 33 arcsec for 2236-176.

gmw_00004536.name: 1ES 1312 423
gmw_00004536.overview: 1ES 1312 423 is a high-frequency peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1310.7736] that is extremely faint in the very-high-energy gamma-ray range [arXiv:1310.7736]. Optical observations reveal a strong positive color-magnitude correlation despite limited flux variations [arXiv:1409.3022].

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1312 423 as a serendipitously discovered HBL detected at VHE, very faint relative to the Crab, and well fit by one-zone SSC modelling.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1312 423 is an HBL which was serendipitously discovered with in the field of view of observations of Cen A. Correcting for the large offset acceptance, the source is detected at the 5.7 CL in 48.4 h. 1ES 1312 423 was found to be very faint in the VHE range, with a flux of 0.5 of the Crab flux, making it one the faintest VHE source currently known. Contemporaneous multi-wavelength data from radio to high energies with ATCA, ATOM, Swift and were used to study its spectral energy distribution, which can be well described using a one-zone SSC model.

sources.summary: The paper finds a strong positive color-magnitude correlation for 1ES 1312--423 despite small flux changes, with optical slope varying between about 2 and 3.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1312--423: The source was observed during 122 nights in 2008--2011. A strong positive CM correlation can be noted despite very limited ( 0.25 mag) flux changes. The optical slope varied between _ BR 2 and 3.

gmw_00004537.name: NGC 3599
gmw_00004537.overview: NGC 3599 is a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1401.4003] that has also been classified as a non-active elliptical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0612340] or a Seyfert-like active galactic nucleus host [arXiv:2109.01092]. The galaxy experienced a slow, soft X-ray flare [arXiv:2108.12431] attributed to either a tidal disruption event [arXiv:2109.01092, arXiv:2108.12431] or an accretion disk instability [arXiv:1509.05193, arXiv:2108.12431].

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 3599, host of XMMJ1115, as Seyfert-like from BPT diagnostics and argues its nucleus hosts a low-luminosity AGN despite WISE colors.

quotes.quote: In this work we investigate the host galaxy of two TDE candidates; the X-ray discovered TDE candidate XMMSL1 J111527.3 + 180638 in the galaxy NGC 3599 and the optically discovered candidate PTF09axc in the galaxy SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2. The nature of the observed flare -- AGN activity or TDE -- has been subject of discussion in the literature for both of these candidates (e.g., Saxton2015 for XMMSL1 J111527.3 + 180638 and Arcavi2014,Jonker2020 for PTF09axc). We aim to classify the host galaxies of two TDE candidates by determining the position of the nuclear emission region on BPT-diagrams. We also look at the WISE colours of the host galaxies and we use the existing L_X [ O iii ] correlation observed in AGNs Heckman2005 to compare the observed [ O iii ] 5007 luminosity to what is predicted on the basis of the correlation.

The position of NGC 3599 on the BPT-diagram suggests it is a Seyfert galaxy, and there have been more papers suggesting the host galaxy is not a quiescent galaxy, see e.g., Saxton2015 . Saxton2015 show that the galaxy was luminous in X-rays 18 months before the peak flux was measured, showing it to be bright on much longer timescales than shown by TDEs known at that point in time. They also argue that even if one of the two measurements was taken during rise-time and one during decay, the rise-time and the plateau phase together would still be significantly longer than seen in previous TDE candidates at that time. Since then, however, longer lived TDE-candidates have been observed (e.g., Lin2017 ) which means the measurements by Saxton2015 can no longer be considered unusual behaviour for a TDE.

sources.summary: The paper reinterprets NGC 3599's soft X-ray flare, arguing its long bright phase rules out a classical fast TDE but supports delayed disruption or an accretion-disc instability.

quotes.quote: Based on new data we have shown that it is difficult to reconcile the rise time of the soft X-ray flare seen in NGC 3599 with its previous classification as a classical, fast-rising, short plateau, tidal disruption event. It does, however, fit into the emerging scheme of a disruption which has led to a late, distant, circularisation where the rise is flatter and the peak lasts longer or alternatively to the tidal stripping of a red giant star. If the factor 100 soft X-ray flare is interpreted as a change in the accretion from a persistent AGN, we find that the rise time of 9 years is too short to be explained purely by the bulk motion of disc material about a 10^ 6 M_ BH.

The observed timescales are compatible with the Lightman-Eardley instability which boosts the thermal soft X-ray emission by heating the inner disk and raising its scale height. This mechanism predicts that flares will repeat on the viscous timescale of the truncation radius, i.e. every few decades. The behaviour of NGC 3599 would then be analogous to flares seen in certain Galactic binary systems, but is too rapid to be equivalent to the state changes which are seen in those systems.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 3599 as a non-active elliptical galaxy whose very soft, high-amplitude X-ray flare and 10^41 erg s^-1 luminosity make it a tidal-disruption candidate.

quotes.quote: In addition, NGC 3599 lies in the field of view of two ROSAT PSPC pointed observations, 600263p and 300169p, allowing a more stringent statement about its high amplitude variability. The corresponding upper limits imply flux ratios of 297 and 100 respectively for this source (note that for the first observation the source is located very close to the PSPC window support structure causing a higher systematic uncertainty).

Although in most cases slew sources do not have enough photons to extract useful X-ray spectra, that of the slew counterpart of the galaxy NGC 3599 demonstrates that the source is very soft (Fig. spect1 ). NGC 3599 is classified as a non-active elliptical galaxy ( ) and SDSS J132341.97+482701.3 is also non-active as its spectrum only shows absorption features from the atmospheres of individual stars (Fig. spect2 ). Therefore, both sources fulfill all properties for tidal disruption events.

It is worth noting that NGC 3599 was observed with an X-ray luminosity of 10^ 41 erg s ^ 1 , which is at the extreme upper end of ultra-luminous X-ray sources (ULX) observed in other galaxies, but an ULX interpretation is unlikely given the very high amplitude of variability detected in this source.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 3599 as a lenticular galaxy formed by a 1:1 major merger, matching its magnitude, SED, morphology, gas content, and stellar ages.

quotes.quote: NGC 3599       The global properties of this lenticular galaxy are well matched by a major merger with a mass ratio of 1:1. The spins of the merging systems are equal ( =0.06, MC03), parallel, both are aligned with the shorter of their principal axes, and perpendicular to the orbital plane (direct encounter). The B band total absolute magnitude, M_B=-18.4 mag, and the SED of the selected snapshot match well with the observed values (Table 1 and Figure figure1 ). The Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) band morphology is compared with the simulated one on the same scale, 5 5 , in Figure figure3 . The age of the galaxy is 12.2 Gyr.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3599 as an analogue for ambiguous TDE or AGN flaring, noting its slow soft X-ray flare, sparse coverage, and possible disk instability.

quotes.quote: NGC 3599 exemplifies this difficulty. This galaxy underwent a slow, soft X-ray flare that rose over multiple years Saxton2015 . It lacked observations between the rise and decay, which complicated the interpretation. However, the slow timescale is atypical of normal, prompt TDEs, although, as we have discussed, it is likely that some TDEs can evolve on much longer timescales and cannot be excluded as a trigger of the flare in NGC 3599. suggested the Lightman-Eardley disk instability as one possible cause of the flare.

gmw_00004538.name: Sacred Mushroom
gmw_00004538.overview: The Sacred Mushroom is an interacting galaxy system [arXiv:1511.05041]. Its structure features a primary disk with a distorted shape and density, as well as a tidal tail that resembles a mushroom stalk [arXiv:1511.05041].

sources.summary: The code paper cites the Sacred Mushroom as a real interacting system modeled by SPAM/JSPAM, reproducing its primary-disk distortion and tidal tail orientation.

quotes.quote: Some of the systems modeled with the precursor SPAM code include the Sacred Mushroom wallin94 also known as AM 1724-622, NGC 2782 (Arp 215) smith94 , and NGC 7714/7715 (Arp 284) smith92 , struck03 . For the Sacred Mushroom we present a DSS image of the system in Figure mimg next to the simulation result in Figure msim . The general shape and density distortion of the primary disk are recreated for the mushroom head, and a tidal tail is formed that matches at least the orientation of the mushroom stalk. The JSPAM code, which preserves the potential used in SPAM, was used to run the simulation shown in Figure msim .

gmw_00004539.name: Arp 86
gmw_00004539.overview: Arp 86 is an interacting luminous infrared galaxy pair consisting of the grand-design galaxy NGC 7753 and its companion NGC 7752 [arXiv:1404.7037]. Tidal interactions have produced extended neutral hydrogen tails, a radio bridge, and active star formation within both galaxies and the connecting bridge [arXiv:1404.7037, arXiv:0904.4908].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Arp 86 as an interacting luminous infrared galaxy pair whose tidal encounter flattened abundance gradients, enhanced star formation, and produced bridges, tails, and possible membership by 2MASX J23470758+2926531.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the metallicity and star formation activities of H ii regions in the interacting system Arp 86, based on the first scientific observation of the multi-object spectroscopy on the 2.16m Telescope at Xinglong Observatory. We find that the oxygen abundance gradient in Arp 86 is flatter than that in normal disk galaxies, which confirms that gas inflows caused by tidal forces during encounters can flatten the metallicity distributions in galaxies.

Arp 86 is an interacting galaxy pair resembling M51 system with a redshift of z 0.0162 from the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED). The galaxy pair consists of a grand-design galaxy NGC 7753, and a small companion NGC 7752. NGC 7753 is a SAB galaxy with the radii R _ 25 1 arcmin (corresponding to 20 kpc), which is defined by the isophote at the brightness of 25 mag/arcsec ^2 in the B-band. A tidal bridge connecting two galaxies is found in optical images and active star formation is lying in two galaxies and the bridge Laurikainen1993 . The infrared luminosities (8 -- 1000 m) of Arp 86 is 10^ 11.01 L_ Sanders2003 , indicating that it is a luminous infrared galaxy (LIRG) system.

sources.summary: The paper presents GMRT H I and radio-continuum observations of Arp86, finding disturbed tidal gas, star-forming high-column-density regions, a radio bridge, and a possible tidal dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: We present the results of Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT) observations of the interacting system Arp86 in both neutral atomic hydrogen, H i , and in radio continuum at 240, 606 and 1394 MHz. In addition to H i emission from the two dominant galaxies, NGC7752 and NGC7753, these observations show a complex distribution of H i tails and bridges due to tidal interactions. The regions of highest column density appear related to the recent sites of intense star formation.

We present the results of the GMRT observations of the interacting system Arp86 in both neutral atomic hydrogen, H i , and in radio continuum at 240, 606 and 1394 MHz. H i maps of the system reveal disturbed morphology with extended tidal arms and debris suggesting an M51-like interaction. The star forming regions in the system seem to correlate well with regions of high H i column density.

sources.summary: The paper uses Arp 86 as a modeled interacting system, saying simulations reproduce its primary galaxy tidal tails and the secondary galaxy's limited distortion.

quotes.quote: Here we present a specific system, Arp 86, that was modeled during the development of that project wallin10 . Figure a86img shows an SDSS image of Arp 86 next to our simulation in Figure a86sim . The general position, orientation, and length of the tidal tails of the primary galaxy are recreated as well as the lack of significant distortion of the secondary galaxy. The length of the northern tail on the primary may appear excessive compared to the grey scale thumbnail from SDSS, but matches the results of n-body simulations salo .

gmw_00004540.name: SDSS J0849+1114
gmw_00004540.overview: SDSS J0849+1114 is a late-stage triple-galaxy merger classified as a luminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:1908.01732]. The system contains three interacting galaxies hosting a triple active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1908.01732], with two of the nuclei exhibiting steep-spectrum radio jets [arXiv:2206.14705].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J0849+1114 as a late-stage triple-galaxy merger and compelling triple AGN, supported by X-ray, optical, near-infrared, and infrared diagnostics.

quotes.quote: In this work we present a suite of multiwavelength observations providing convincing evidence for a triple AGN in the merging system, SDSS J0849+1114. SDSS J0849+1114 is a late stage merger at a distance of 350 Mpc ( z =0.077 ) comprising three interacting galaxies, with nuclear pair separations of 2.3 (3.4 kpc, Galaxy \ 1 Galaxy \ 2 ), 3.6 (5.3 kpc, Galaxy \ 1 Galaxy \ 3 ), and 5.0 (7.3 kpc, Galaxy \ 2 Galaxy \ 3 ) based upon archival black (PI: X. Liu) Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) imaging data (Figure fig:hst_xrays ). Morphologically, the system exhibits strong tidal features indicative of an advanced merger. With an integrated 8-1000 m infrared luminosity of log ( L _ IR / L _ )=11.43 0.03 , this merger falls within the class of Luminous Infrared Galaxies (LIRGs).

As reported in , SDSS J0849+1114 exhibited three nuclear X-ray sources with luminosities in excess of that expected by star formation contributions, and a high ionization [ SiVI ] coronal emission line was identified in the near-infrared spectrum of Galaxy 1. Furthermore, the system exhibited signs of high absorption along the line of sight based on its X-ray spectral properties.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA imaging of SDSS J0849+1114, detecting steep-spectrum radio emission from nuclei A and C, resolving AGN-driven jet/outflow structures, and setting strict non-detection limits for nucleus B.

quotes.quote: In this work we present a multi-band (3.0, 6.0 10.0, and 15.0 GHz), high-resolution radio imaging of the triple AGN candidate, SDSS J0849+1114, using the Very Large Array.
