Two of the three nuclei (A and C) are detected at 3.0, 6.0, and 15 GHz for the first time, both exhibiting a steep spectrum over 3--15 GHz (with a spectral index 0.90 0.05 and 1.03 0.04 ) consistent with a synchrotron origin. Nucleus A, the strongest nucleus among the three, shows a double-sided jet, with the jet orientation changing by 20 between its inner 1 '' and the outer 5 5 (8.1 kpc) components, which may be explained as the MBH's angular momentum having been altered by merger-enhanced accretion. Nucleus C also shows a two-sided jet, with the western jet inflating into a radio lobe with an extent of 1 5 (2.2 kpc). The internal energy of the radio lobe is estimated to be 5.0 10^ 55 erg, for an equipartition magnetic field strength of 160 \ G .

No significant radio emission is detected at all four frequencies for nucleus B, yielding an upper limit of 15, 15, 15, and 18 Jy \ beam^ 1 at 3.0, 6.0, 10.0, and 15.0 GHz, based on which we constrain the star formation rate in nucleus B to be 0.4 M_ yr^ 1 .

gmw_00004541.name: SDSS J090331+010847
gmw_00004541.overview: SDSS J090331+010847 is a massive radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.522 [arXiv:2208.04569]. The galaxy hosts a compact, peaked-spectrum radio source and exhibits associated H I absorption with an exceptionally high optical depth [arXiv:2208.04569].

sources.summary: The FLASH pilot paper reports associated H I absorption in SDSS J090331+010847, a massive radio galaxy with a peaked radio spectrum, quenched star formation, and exceptionally high optical depth.

quotes.quote: We detected an associated H i absorption line towards the radio galaxy SDSS J090331+010847 at redshift z=0.522 ching2017 . We show the full-band radio spectrum for this object (from SBID 13283) in Fig. fig:J090331+010847_full_band_spectrum . The quality of the spectrum is good and the band is free of radio frequency interference (RFI), though a few glitches are seen, notably between 916 and 920 MHz. These are caused by a `correlator dropout', an intermittent ASKAP issue in which some of the high-resolution frequency channels in a single correlator block cease to provide data. An absorption line is seen at 933.352 MHz which is consistent with the GAMA optical redshift of SDSS J090331+010847.

Both these galaxies are massive (stellar mass >10^ 11 M _ at z 0.5 ) and contain compact, peaked-spectrum radio sources. These are relatively faint radio sources, with flux densities of 55 and 171 mJy respectively at 855.5 MHz (63 and 155 mJy at 1.4 GHz). Both absorption lines have high optical depth, 1.77^ +0.16 _ 0.16 for SDSS J090331+010847 and 0.14^ +0.01 _ 0.01 (from 6 hours observation with SBID 13306) for SDSS J113622+004852, implying column densities above 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 .

gmw_00004542.name: SDSS J113622+004852
gmw_00004542.overview: SDSS J113622+004852 is a massive, gas-rich, and dust-obscured starburst galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.563 [arXiv:2208.04569]. It exhibits associated H I absorption, a peaked radio spectrum, and a sustained high star-formation rate [arXiv:2208.04569].

sources.summary: The paper reports associated H I absorption in SDSS J113622+004852, finding a massive dust-obscured starburst, peaked radio spectrum, high gas column, and sustained high star formation.

quotes.quote: We also detected an associated H i absorption line towards the galaxy SDSS J113622+004852 at redshift z=0.563 . The full-band radio spectrum from SBID 13306 is shown in Fig. fig:J113622+004852_full_band_spectrum and has an absorption line at 908.63 MHz. The absorption redshift is consistent with the GAMA optical redshift, implying associated absorption. An enlarged spectrum with rms noise of 2.98 mJy beam ^ 1 channel ^ 1 is shown in Fig. fig:optical_radio_plot and is best fitted with a single Gaussian component.

The high H i column density measured from H i absorption and the properties derived from the UV-optical-infrared SED imply that SDSS J113622+004852 is a massive, gas-rich, and dust-obscured starburst galaxy. From the SED-derived dust mass, we estimate an mass of at least 10^ 11 M _ for J113622+004852. In contrast to J090331+010847, the fit for SDSS J113622+004852 implies that it has sustained a high star-formation rate since at least z 2 and is continuing to form stars at the rate of over 50 M_ yr^ 1 with no sign of quenching. Its recent star formation history appears to be very different from that of J090331+010847, implying that the relationship between and star formation at intermediate redshift is far from straightforward.

gmw_00004543.name: NGC 271
gmw_00004543.overview: NGC 271 is classified as an early-type barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0308491]. It features a well-defined bar pattern speed and has available global neutral hydrogen profiles [arXiv:astro-ph/0308491].

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 271 in its Tremaine-Weinberg barred-galaxy sample, finding a well-defined pattern speed and estimating circular velocity, asymmetric drift, and bar length.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present an analysis of the bar pattern speed in four barred galaxies using the TW method. Three galaxies, NGC 271, NGC 1358 and ESO 281-31 are early type barred galaxies.

NGC 271 The data of this galaxy are consistent with a well-defined pattern speed in this galaxy. Global HI profiles are available for NGC 271 and NGC 1358 (Theureau 1998) and can in principle be used to estimate the circular velocities, assuming the rotation curve at large radii is flat. K band photometry for NGC 271 was obtained from the Two Micron All Sky Survey (2mass).

gmw_00004544.name: ESO 281-31
gmw_00004544.overview: ESO 281-31 is an early-type barred galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0308491]. Longslit spectral data for the galaxy are consistent with a well-defined bar pattern speed [arXiv:astro-ph/0308491].

sources.summary: The paper treats ESO 281-31 as an early-type barred galaxy with extensive longslit data, a well-defined pattern speed, and bar-length analysis from I-band photometry.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present an analysis of the bar pattern speed in four barred galaxies using the TW method. Three galaxies, NGC 271, NGC 1358 and ESO 281-31 are early type barred galaxies.

ESO 281-31 More longslit spectra have been obtained for this galaxy than for the other galaxies observed with the NTT to facilitate a detailed comparison with the stellar kinematics derived from a complete Fabry-Perot data set in a forthcoming study. The longslit data are consistent with a well-defined pattern speed in this galaxy. I band images of ESO 281-31 and NGC 3992 were kindly made available by Victor Debattista and Roelof Bottema respectively.

gmw_00004545.name: 6C0905
gmw_00004545.overview: 6C0905 is a high-redshift giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.88 [arXiv:0803.1545, arXiv:astro-ph/0605391]. Classified as a classical double Fanaroff-Riley II source [arXiv:1107.0824, arXiv:astro-ph/0605391], it exhibits extended X-ray emission along its radio axis produced by inverse-Compton scattering within its lobes [arXiv:0803.1545, arXiv:astro-ph/0605391].

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM observations of 6C0905, a high-redshift giant radio galaxy whose extended X-rays are interpreted as inverse-Compton emission from old radio lobes.

quotes.quote: At redshift z=1.88 , \ is one of the highest redshift giant radio galaxies known. It spans 111 \ on the sky lawgreen95 , which represents a projected size of 945 \ in the cosmology assumed in this paper. 6c0905 observed this powerful, classical double \ FR radio galaxy with \ and detected extended X-ray emission along the axis of the radio source which we concluded was emitted by inverse-Compton scattering of the Cosmic Microwave Background (ICCMB, CMB) by the lobes (undetected in the radio) of the radio galaxy.

Our \ data clearly show X-ray emission extending along the radio axis of \ to the east and west (Fig. fig:colour ). This was first detected by \ and presented in 6c0905 where we noted that, as \ resolves the transverse extent of this extended emission ( 40 , although the paucity of counts makes the difficult to measure this accurately), it cannot be jet emission (since this would be very narrow) but rather is due to inverse-Compton up-scattering of the CMB by spent radio plasma from the lobes. The \ data supports this interpretation.

sources.summary: The paper models 6C 0905+3955 as a powerful z=1.88 FR II galaxy, using its X-ray lobe emission, radio luminosity, and size to constrain hotspot low-energy cutoffs.

quotes.quote: We also apply the model to match the features of 6C 0905+3955, a classical double FR II galaxy thought to have a low-energy cutoff of 10^4 in the hotspot due to a lack of hotspot inverse-Compton X-ray emission. The models suggest that the low-energy cutoff in the hotspots of 6C 0905+3955 is 10^3 , just slightly above the particles required for X-ray emission.

Previous 6C 0905+3955 observations The powerful z=1.88 FR II source 6C 0905+3955 was most recently observed in the X-ray by XMM-Newton 2008MNRAS.386.1774E . The projected size of the source is 945 kpc 2006ApJ...644L..13B with an axial ratio of 8 , although the source has arm-length asymmetry (the ratio of arm lengths is 1.6 ) 1995MNRAS.277..995L,2006ApJ...644L..13B . The extended X-ray lobe emission was modelled with a photon index of =1.61 and has a 2 - 10 keV luminosity of 1.5 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 2008MNRAS.386.1774E . The MERLIN 408 MHz image gives a radio luminosity of the lobes of 8.4 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 1995MNRAS.277..995L .

sources.summary: The paper studies 6C 0905+3955 as a high-redshift FRII giant radio galaxy, measuring its nearly-Mpc radio structure and extended X-ray emission from aged lobe plasma.

quotes.quote: A particularly important target amongst these is the powerful classical double FRII Fan74 radio galaxy 6C 0905+3955 (also known as 4C39.24), at the relatively high-redshift of z = 1.88 Law95 . This object has a physical size projected on the plane of the sky of 945 kpc [ using the assumed cosmology of H_ 0 = 71 _ M =0.27 and _ = 0.73 and its measured angular size of 111 ^ ] . The radio emission from 6C 0905+3955, shown in Figure fig:overlay , and indeed at 408 MHz in figure 1a of Law95 which closely resembles our image at 1.4 GHz, represents the entireity of the radio emission at these frequencies: the radio lobes seen just inward of the compact hotspots, together with the core, have the same silhouette at low frequency Law95 as our high frequency image in Figure fig:overlay shows.

The \ X-ray satellite observed 6C 0905+3955 on 2005 March 02: the X-ray emission from this object is well-detected in 19.68 \ with about 114 \ in total (all counts quoted are background subtracted), and is spread in a linear fashion contained within the giant radio structure (Figure fig:overlay ).

The extended structures of the giant radio galaxy 6C 0905+3955 in X-rays and radio have been compared and show that the bulk of the X-ray emission associated with non-compact components (i.e. \ lobe material) preferentially occurs where the synchrotron plasma is expected to be oldest.

gmw_00004546.name: LRG
gmw_00004546.overview: The LRG is a luminous red galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.5604 [arXiv:1304.0006]. Its spectrum is dominated by absorption features, indicating an old underlying stellar population with very little recent star formation [arXiv:1304.0006].

sources.summary: The LRG lies near the ultra-strong Mg II absorber and is treated as a likely massive group member with old stellar populations and a possibly bound gaseous halo.

quotes.quote: This absorber was originally found at projected separation =246 kpc of a luminous red galaxy (LRG) at z=0.5604 . This projected separation is less than the virial radius ( 500 kpc) of LRGs at z 0.5 (eg., \ blake2008a,gauthier2009a ) while | v| is comparable to the virial velocity of LRGs suggesting that the absorbing gas could be gravitationally bound to the dark matter halo of the LRG. The LRG has i'=19.6 corresponding to 4 L_* .

Galaxy A and the LRG exhibit spectra features dominated by absorption transitions, indicating an old underlying stellar population and little star formation in the recent past. The distribution of SFR _ cont confirms the old nature of the stellar populations of galaxies A and the LRG.

gmw_00004547.name: 3C 47
gmw_00004547.overview: 3C 47 is a radio-loud, steep-spectrum quasar characterized by an extremely jetted structure [arXiv:2402.14094, arXiv:astro-ph/0006315]. Its double-peaked emission lines are well modeled by a relativistic Keplerian accretion disk [arXiv:2402.14094], while its infrared emission remains effectively constant [arXiv:astro-ph/0006315].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 47 as an extremely jetted, radio-loud quasar whose double-peaked emission lines are well explained by a relativistic Keplerian accretion disk.

quotes.quote: This work aims to address whether the AD can account for the double-peaked profiles observed in the Balmer lines ( , ), near-UV ( ), and high-ionization UV lines ( , ) of the extremely jetted quasar 3C 47 . The low ionization lines (LILs) ( , , and ) were analyzed using a relativistic Keplerian AD model. Fits were carried out following Bayesian and multicomponent non-linear approaches.

The LIL profiles of 3C 47 are in very good agreement with a relativistic Keplerian AD model. The disk emission is constrained between 10^2 and 10^3 \ gravitational radii, with a viewing angle of 30 degrees. The main alternative, a double broad line region associated with a binary black hole, is found to be less appealing than the disk model for the quasar 3C 47 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 47 as a steep-spectrum radio-loud quasar whose IR emission is effectively constant; radio variability is too small to explain the stronger IR flux, supporting an additional component.

quotes.quote: For two other sources (3C 47, and B2 2201+31A) the observed variation is only marginally < 1.6 , where includes the statistical and the systematic uncertainties. We consider their emission as constant in the span of our observations.

The variability factors observed in two SSRQ in our sample, 3C 47 and PG 1004+130, are too small to explain the much higher IR fluxes, and this is probably true for the SSRQ in general. In the mm domain we measured a flux variation from the core of the SSRQ 3C 47 of a factor of 2 in almost three years (the emitted flux density at 100 GHz was equal to 16.3 0.9 mJy on September 1995 ( ), and equal to 30.8 0.6 mJy on July 1998 (this work)).

gmw_00004548.name: UDSR09
gmw_00004548.overview: UDSR09 is a luminous infrared galaxy located at an intermediate redshift of z ~ 0.4 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509867]. It exhibits early-type optical properties and Seyfert 2 emission-line diagnostics, with a high star formation rate and X-ray emission dominated by an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0509867].

sources.summary: The paper studies UDSR09 as a 15 micron-selected Marano Field galaxy with early-type optical properties, Seyfert 2 emission-line diagnostics, high infrared star formation, and unusually strong X-ray emission dominated by an AGN.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we study the properties of one 15 m-selected galaxy, UDSR09, in this field. We present its high quality VLT/FORS2 optical spectrum and ISOCAM MIR data, associated with the X-ray and radio data from literature. The meaning of UDSR09 marks the ninth slit of the FORS2 spectroscopic observation in the Ultra-Deep-Survey-ROSAT field (Liang et al. 2004).
This galaxy is a luminous infrared galaxy in the z 0.4 intermediate redshift Universe, with a high SFR of 34 M_ yr ^ 1 .

The [ OIII ] , [ OII ] and H emission lines diagnostic diagram shows its Seyfert 2 galaxy property. Its IR and radio data relation follows the radio-IR correlations of local and ISOCAM intermediate- z galaxies. However, its X-ray luminosity is much higher than that of general early-type galaxies at the given B band luminosity, and much higher than the star forming tracer, FIR luminositys, which means that the sources of its X-ray emission should not come from stellar components, and the AGN is the dominate component.

gmw_00004549.name: J130724.08+460400.9
gmw_00004549.overview: J130724.08+460400.9 is a galaxy pair consisting of two clearly interacting, widely separated bright galaxies [arXiv:1102.1733]. This system is undergoing a merger event, with the point source contributing less than 10 percent of the total light of the larger galaxy [arXiv:1102.1733].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J130724.08+460400.9 as a dual-structure QSO host: a clearly interacting, widely separated galaxy pair with low point-source contribution from the larger galaxy.

quotes.quote: Our images resolve structure on scales of a kpc or less in all these galaxies. A large fraction of our targets appear to have companions or show signs of being in a galaxy merger, highlighting the value of kinematic methods to find AGN in mergers. Of the twelve, three are in pairs with fairly equal H-band luminosity ratio: J095207+255257, J130724+460400 and J124859-025730.

The obvious signatures of an interaction in these systems strongly suggest that they are undergoing a merger event. J020011-093126 has a minor companion, but a low surface brightness bridge connects the two galaxies. We will refer to these systems as `mergers' in the rest of the paper.
The first, J130724.08+460400.9, is a pair of clearly interacting widely separated bright galaxies and the point source contributes less than 10 \ of the total light of the larger galaxy.

gmw_00004550.name: J091649.41+000031.5
gmw_00004550.overview: J091649.41+000031.5 is an elliptical galaxy that hosts a quasar [arXiv:1102.1733]. The galaxy exhibits a fairly smooth core, and its H-band light is not dominated by the active galactic nucleus point source [arXiv:1102.1733].

sources.summary: The paper describes J091649.41+000031.5 as an elliptical QSO host with a smooth core, uncertain point-source fraction, and H-band light not dominated by the AGN point source.

quotes.quote: The image of the second case, J091649.41+000031.5, shows an elliptical galaxy with a fairly smooth core and the GALFIT modeling suggests a negligible amount of light from a point source. However, our PSF monitoring images, taken just after the images of the galaxy, show that the performance of the AO system suffered significant variation during this phase of the night. A broad PSF, probably due to an unsettled low bandwidth wavefront sensor in the AO system, would smooth out the core of the image and lower the contrast of a point source.
Therefore, we are unable to accurately estimate the point source fraction in this galaxy, but can conclude that it does not dominate the H-band light.
We have excluded J091649.41+000031.5, as the point source fraction is very uncertain for this particular galaxy (see 3).

gmw_00004551.name: LBG2
gmw_00004551.overview: LBG2 is a Lyman-break galaxy candidate located near the quasar ULAS J1120+0641 [arXiv:1406.0851]. Spectral energy distribution fitting suggests that the source is most likely a galaxy at a redshift of approximately 2 [arXiv:1406.0851].

sources.summary: The paper evaluates LBG2 as a candidate dropout near ULAS J1120+0641, fits its SED with IRAC and HST photometry, and concludes it is most likely a z~2 galaxy.

quotes.quote: Inspection of these revealed a source offset by 0.4 0.2 arcsec to the southwest of LBG2, i.e., away from the bright galaxy seen at the top of the postage stamps in Fig.  fig:postages , but no detections at the locations of the other LBG candidates (Table  tab:irac ). We attempted to determine the nature of LBG2 and the nearby galaxy by fitting their spectral energy distributions using the photometric redshift code EAzY (Brammer, van Dokkum Coppi 2008). We assume that all the IRAC flux arises from LBG2 and supplement the HST photometry of the nearby galaxy with data from Subaru/Suprime-Cam observations in the i and z filters.

Using simple stellar populations from Bruzual Charlot (2003) attenuated by Pei's (1992) parametrization of the Small Magellanic Cloud extinction law, we obtain photometric redshifts of z=2.49^ +0.14 _ 1.05 for LBG2 and z=0.27^ +0.17 _ 0.18 for the nearby galaxy. There is a strong anti-correlation between redshift and extinction in the fit to LBG2, so lower redshift solutions require more heavily-reddened populations (see Fig.  fig:lbg2 ). All fits to LBG2 predict Y_ 105 J_ 125 1.1 , redder than observed, but consistent with the data within 3 . These fits suggest that the IRAC photometry of LBG2 is contaminated by the nearby galaxy at a level of only a few per cent, which does not significantly affect the fit.
We therefore conclude that LBG2 is most likely to be a galaxy at z 2 and the probability of our finding only two or fewer z 7 galaxies in our field is 7 per cent.

gmw_00004552.name: VTC221515-005028
gmw_00004552.overview: VTC221515-005028 is a faint red galaxy [arXiv:1601.01693] classified as a low-luminosity, radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1601.01693]. The source exhibits a flat radio spectrum [arXiv:1601.01693] alongside extreme optical and radio variability [arXiv:1601.01693].

sources.summary: The paper identifies VTC221515-005028 as a faint red galaxy and extremely variable flat-spectrum, low-luminosity radio-loud AGN with radio and optical variability.

quotes.quote: VTC221515-005028 has flux densities 2.569 0.086, 1.989 0.062, 1.778 0.071, 1.787 0.058 mJy and spectral indices 0.24 0.30 , 0.03 0.31 , 0.68 0.38 , and 0.45 0.72 in epochs E1--E4 of our survey. It is absent in the FIRST survey (mean epoch 1996.1), where the flux density at that location is 0.25 0.10 mJy, but present in the 1.4 GHz Stripe 82 survey catalog hodge2011 with a peak flux density of 0.79 mJy.
The source appears to have a flat spectrum between 1--5 GHz and a spectral index of 1.1 between 5--15 GHz.

The optical counterpart of VTC221515-005028 is a faint red galaxy having 20.8 mag. From the PTF light curve it is seen to exhibit stochastic variability with a maximum amplitude of one magnitude in the R band, and having a shortest timescale of about three days. The SDSS light curve between 1999--2008 reveals sub-magnitude variability and intermittent flares with amplitudes upto =2 mag on 1 day timescale. The photometric redshift from SDSS is 0.37. The radio-to-optical flux density ratio for the host galaxy, R=log(S_ 1.4 GHz /S_g) 2.8 , and the radio luminosity at 1.4 GHz, 9.0 10^ 29   erg s ^ 1 Hz ^ 1 , suggest a low-luminosity radio-loud AGN. We thus conclude that VTC221515-005028 is an extremely variable flat-spectrum AGN.

gmw_00004553.name: eFEDSU J091157.5+014327
gmw_00004553.overview: eFEDSU J091157.5+014327 is an obscured quasar located at a redshift of z=0.603 [arXiv:2106.14525]. It exhibits a high X-ray luminosity, moderate obscuration, and an outflow, which are consistent with a quasar in a merger-driven feedback phase [arXiv:2106.14525].

sources.summary: The paper presents eFEDSU J091157.5+014327 as an obscured feedback-phase quasar with broad [OIII], high X-ray luminosity, moderate obscuration, outflow signatures, and possible merger environment.

quotes.quote: Only one source (eFEDSU J091157.5+014327) has a spectrum already available (from SDSS-DR16, z=0.603) and it unambiguously shows the presence of a broad component (FWHM 1650 km/s) in the [ OIII ] 5007 line.

The associated observed L _ [ OIII ] is 2.6 10^ 42 erg/s, one to two orders of magnitude larger than that observed in local Seyferts and comparable to those observed in a sample of z 0.5 Type 1 Quasars. From the multiwavelength data available we derive an Eddington Ratio (L _ bol /L _ Edd ) of 0.25, and a bolometric correction in the hard X-ray band of k _ bol 10 , lower than those observed for objects at similar bolometric luminosity. These properties, along with the presence of an outflow, the high X-ray luminosity and moderate X-ray obscuration (L _ X 10^ 44.8 erg/s, N _H 2.7 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ) and the red optical color, all match the prediction of quasars in the feedback phase from merger driven models.

Of these three eROSITA objects, only one source has a spectroscopic redshift already available from SDSS DR16 (Lyke et al. 2020), eFEDSU J091157.5+014327 at z=0.603 (XID439 in the eFEDS catalog, shown as a red star in Figure  FigSel ; Nandra et al. submitted). X-ray spectrum Bayesian spectral analysis with automatic background fitting (Simmonds et al., 2018) was performed on the eROSITA spectra extracted in the 0.2-10 keV bands of all the eFEDS point-like sources with BXA (Buchner et al. 2014), which connects XSPEC (Arnaud 1996) with the UltraNest https://github.com/JohannesBuchner/UltraNest/ nested sampling algorithm (Buchner 2016, 2019). Several models have been adopted (single and double power-law model, thermal model, and, for the sources with the lowest statistics, models with fixed photon index). All the details of the spectral fitting are reported in Liu et al. (submitted). The benefit of nested sampling includes that the procedure automatically characterizes all posterior modes, measures the uncertainties and multi-variate degeneracies, identifies its convergence automatically, and enables Bayesian model comparison among different models. A main benefit for large surveys is that nested sampling can be run unsupervised on each source, regardless of spectral quality, and produces homogeneous output products. Figure 2 shows the eROSITA total and background spectra, with the best fit model of an absorbed power law at the redshift of the source. Adopting a uniform prior for the slope and a logarithmic prior for N _H , the best fit photon index and column density are =2.27 ^ +0.39 _ 0.44 and N _H =2.75 0.65 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , respectively. We also fit the X-ray spectrum with an absorbed thermal model at the redshift of the source ( tbabs*apec ), which returned a best fit temperature of kT 3.5 keV and a column density of 8 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 , with a statistical significance similar to the absorbed power-law fit. However, these physical properties are hardly justified when compared to other properties of the system (see Section 2.2). We therefore consider the absorbed power-law model the one which best reproduces our data. The intrinsic 2-10 keV luminosity from the best fit model is L _X=6.5^ +1.15 _ 1.0 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 . From an X--ray point of view the source is therefore classified as an obscured QSO, confirming the effectiveness of the selection criterion in revealing these rare systems.

gmw_00004554.name: SDSS J1402+2631
gmw_00004554.overview: SDSS J1402+2631 is a quasar located at a redshift of approximately z = 0.188 [arXiv:2010.09735]. Its spectrum displays broad Paschen emission lines, including a broad Paschen-alpha component with a velocity width of 3300 kilometers per second [arXiv:2010.09735].

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J1402+2631 as a quasar SMBH-binary astrometry case study, measuring its Pa profile and simulating GRAVITY+ differential phase signals.

quotes.quote: As one example, we consider the object SDSS J1402+2631. We have measured the Pa emission line profile of this quasar ( fig:gplus_phase ) using the TripleSpec instrument at the Apache Point Observatory 3.5m telescope. Observations were taken in June 2020 with the 1.1 slit in a standard nodding ABBA sequence of 8 120 s exposures.

We detect broad emission lines of Pa , , , , at a redshift of z 0.188 in the JHK band spectra. The continuum flux corresponds to K = 12.8 , similar to the K = 12.5 measured by 2MASS. fig:gplus_phase shows a decomposition of the Pa emission line into Gaussian broad and narrow components, where the broad line component has a velocity width 3300 km s^ 1 and peak relative line strength of f 0.12 .
